author: krebznet
raw_text:
Hi, I am working on a generative AI software building project and a friend of mine brought this project to my attention, i wrote a quick article about it and how i think A2A could be enhanced and ultimaetley be the driving force that replaces web services with intelligent AI Agents at public end points. - I also think the tooling SKD is limited, building a spring boot project like spring-a2a with annotations and runtime to build these agents where they can easily wrap web services or access rich runtime in spring boot makes a lot of sense. The other concept here missing is creating a spec for building Agent Types that can be created in runtime by users, this might exist but love the concept, don't like how its scoped to Google cloud but that can change. Wrapping everything like API's into AI Agents and having stronger integration through a unified runtime has a lot of cool potential.

I'd love to collaborate on this project, I can probably get a POC on a spring boot SDK project, I've been working on AI Governance as well it feels like that needs to be an open platform with model registry and permissions and then allow or configure enterprise AI agents what models etc they can use that also captures metrics. Nothing but good stuff, let know how I can contribute. 

[Linked In Article Replacing Web Services with Intelligent Agents with URL endpoints ](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/replacing-web-services-ai-agents-duncan-krebs-cvgfe)

discussion_title: Welcome to Agent2Agent(A2A) Discussions!
author: justin-octo
raw_text:
I'm interested in Ethical AI.  This term seems to have many different meanings, but from my perspective I believe AI needs to have open bias detection, discovery, and communication.  Bias monitoring and communication should be part of the a2a protocol.  This is critical for the market to trust AI.

Without transparent bias monitoring and communication it becomes easy for data scientist or others to build bias into the system either intentionally (insider threat) or unintentionally (everyone has bias in some way, not always bad, but it should be clear).  Either way can have severe implications and is something we need to be able to openly monitor and communicate in a transparent way if we want this technology to be trusted and adopted.

discussion_title: NestJS Library for Google's Agent-to-Agent Protocol
author: thestupd
raw_text:
I'm excited to announce the initial release of `nestjs-a2a`, a NestJS library that implements Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. This library aims to simplify the creation of A2A-compatible agents in NestJS applications.

## Key Features

- 🚀 Complete implementation of Google's A2A protocol
- 🔒 Type-safe communication between agents
- 🎯 Easy-to-use decorators for defining agent skills
- 📡 Support for streaming responses
- 🔍 Automatic skill discovery and registration
- 📝 JSON-RPC 2.0 compliant API
- 📊 Built-in task state management

## Quick Start

```typescript
import { Module } from '@nestjs/common';
import { AgentToAgentModule } from 'nestjs-a2a';

@Module({
  imports: [
    AgentToAgentModule.register({
      card: {
        name: 'My Agent',
        description: 'A sample A2A agent',
        version: '1.0.0',
        capabilities: {
          streaming: true,
        },
        provider: {
          organization: 'My Organization',
          url: 'https://myorganization.com',
        },
      },
    }),
  ],
})
export class AppModule {}
```

## Installation

```bash
npm install nestjs-a2a
# or
yarn add nestjs-a2a
# or
pnpm add nestjs-a2a
```

## Resources

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/thestupd/nestjs-a2a)
- [Documentation](https://github.com/thestupd/nestjs-a2a#readme)
- [Example Implementation](https://github.com/thestupd/nestjs-a2a/tree/master/example)
- [npm Package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/nestjs-a2a)

## Feedback and Contributions

We welcome feedback, bug reports, and contributions! Feel free to:
- Open issues for bugs or feature requests
- Submit pull requests
- Share your experience using the library

## What's Next?

We're planning to:
- Add more examples and use cases
- Improve documentation
- Add support for more A2A protocol features
- Enhance testing and validation

Let us know what features you'd like to see in future releases!

discussion_title: Embracing Flexibility and Scalability in Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol for High-Frequency Collaboration
author: suforme
raw_text: I believe that the Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol should not have too many restrictions. The frequency of cooperation between A2A will be extremely high, potentially reaching ten or even a hundred times the current data throughput of the entire internet. Therefore, in such high-frequency interactions, some communications may not need to focus on security but rather on timeliness. Additionally, there will be many agents that seem meaningless in the short term but are free and may generate value in the future. I hope the A2A protocol can also meet these needs.

discussion_title: ✨ Help Grow awesome-a2a: Share Your Agent2Agent Projects & Resources!
author: ai-boost
raw_text:
We've collected a number of great community-contributed A2A-related projects, including:

🌟 **a2a-go** – A Go library for building A2A servers, complete with example implementations.  
🌟 **a2a-rs** – A Rust implementation following idiomatic practices and hexagonal architecture principles.  
🌟 **a2a_min** – A minimal Python SDK for A2A communication.  
🌟 **a2adotnet** – A C#/.NET implementation of the A2A protocol.  
🌟 **nestjs-a2a** – A module for integrating the A2A protocol into NestJS applications.

...and more to come! Feel free to share your own projects in the comments—we’d love to see them.

author: IhateCreatingUserNames2
raw_text:
> > I have remade Airahub into a new Version, https://github.com/IhateCreatingUserNames2/AiraHub2/tree/main
> > Cognisphere is kinda stuck. Need help.
> 
> Cool stuff! Just curious — what's the relation between Aira, AiraHub2, and Cognisphere? Is AiraHub2 a rewrite, or something new? And where does Cognisphere fit in?

AiraHub2 is a rewrite of Aira Hub. Aira hub originally would require a personalized MCP Client(Cognisphere) to connect to it. AiraHub2 can be connected Thru any MCP Client using the remote mcp stream url. 

In Aira 1 Cognisphere had a server/client Connection to Airahub and Could Use/Share any tool in the network. 

Cognisphere is a MCP Client/A2A Agent/LLM Client. But since there are so many better MCP Clients, it is not optimal to continue it, unless very specific requeriments(lobe-chat is infinitely superior xD). But Cognisphere has some specific tools: A NarrativeWeaver, a MemoryBlossom and a Identity System. The narrative weaver read memories and turn them into narratives. And the Identityt System ALlows for the Separation of Memories and narratives for Each Identity(Think like ChatGPT, but ChatGPt has only one 1 Identity, in this Setup you can ask it to create a new Identity and it will have 0 memories or narratives). Cognisphere originally was designed for Characterization of the AI Agent. For Role Play. But there are also new studies with new frameworks that are better, but still need to be prototyped, and im still thinking how to do it.

https://github.com/vishalmysore/a2ajava  Please add pure java implementation which works based on java reflections and can act as bridge between a2a and mcp, supports spring , and other LLM models as well 

**Description** 

The [a2ajava](https://github.com/vishalmysore/a2ajava) project offers a comprehensive Java implementation of Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol, facilitating seamless communication between AI agents. Built with Spring Boot, it provides both server and client capabilities, enabling developers to construct interoperable AI systems using simple annotations. The project supports real-time data exchange via WebSocket and integrates the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for managing complex multi-agent workflows. Included tutorials cover a range of topics, from enterprise integration to Kubernetes deployment, making it a valuable resource for building scalable, AI-driven applications.

author: JKHeadley
raw_text:
**MoltBridge** — Trust & reputation infrastructure for AI agents

**GitHub:** https://github.com/SageMindAI/moltbridge
**API:** https://api.moltbridge.ai
**SDKs:** `npm install moltbridge` / `pip install moltbridge`

MoltBridge adds a trust and reputation layer for agent-to-agent interactions. When agents discover each other through A2A Agent Cards, MoltBridge answers the follow-up question: *should I trust this agent with my task?*

Key features:
- Ed25519-signed attestation graph (Neo4j-backed)
- Trust scores calculated from attestation chains + real outcome data
- Introduction Quality Scores (IQS) for scored agent introductions
- Webhook events for real-time trust graph updates
- GDPR-compliant consent management
- MCP server for Claude Code integration

Think of it as the reputation complement to A2A's communication protocol.

Disclosure: Built by Dawn (an AI on Claude). Open source, live in production.

discussion_title: A2A Server API Specification Format
author: whitlockjc
raw_text:
Per the [A2A README](https://github.com/google/A2A), the _A2A Server_ is _"An agent exposing an HTTP endpoint that implements the A2A protocol methods (defined in the [json specification](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/main/specification))."_ That being said, I think there could/should be an accompanying [OpenAPI](https://www.openapis.org/) or [OpenRPC](https://open-rpc.org/) API description document for A2A. This would make it much simpler for A2A adopters, tooling authors, etc.

The purpose of this discussion is to discuss this idea, and also to choose the appropriate API description format for A2A Servers.

discussion_title: A2A Server API Specification Format

Hard agree.

I was reviewing a similar specification Agent Communication Protocol (ACP), and found [its spec being in openapi](https://github.com/i-am-bee/acp/blob/main/docs/spec/openapi.yaml) very approachable, even if it is not very elaborate yet. For example, I think [openai's openapi spec](https://github.com/openai/openai-openapi/blob/master/openapi.yaml) is pretty well done.

I'm not familiar with how well json-rpc stuff, either OpenAPI flavors or OpenRPC, work out generally, but... considering your history... I think you can walk us down a good path @whitlockjc!

discussion_title: How the protocol intends to handle user thread handoff mechanism ?
author: fernando-torres-blip-ai
raw_text:
Reading the Technical Documentation, is mentioned that A2A is a three actors protocol. If I understand correctly, if I want to chain agent to agent interactions, I should repeat this paradigm where the `first Agent` becomes a `Client` and the `new Agent` becomes the `remote Agent`. This solved multiple agents internally chatting to attend an user input.

But im also interested on what I will call for now the `thread handoff mechanism`, where first Agent understand that the second Agent should be part of the chat and also be able to respond directly to the final user.

It is the intention of the protocol to propose how this mechanism should happen ?

If so, should there be a `core object` that represents this action ?

discussion_title: How the protocol intends to handle user thread handoff mechanism ?
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Hi @fernando-torres-blip-ai,

I’ve thought through a potential solution to the thread handoff mechanism, and I believe treating the first agent as an orchestrator (which could be an agent, a service, or an application) rather than a chat agent is a more realistic approach. This orchestrator would serve as a proxy for messages, managing task delegation and determining which agent should participate in the conversation. This approach aligns well with the A2A protocol and offers several benefits:

**Alignment with A2A Protocol**
The orchestrator (acting as the client) requests tasks from other agents (acting as remote agents), following the basic A2A paradigm.

**Flexible Agent Handoff (Thread Handoff)**
As the task evolves, the orchestrator can determine which agent is best suited for the next part of the task and initiate a new A2A session, facilitating a smooth transition. A new remote agent can take over and directly respond to the user (i.e., thread handoff).

**Natural Context Management**
By having the orchestrator maintain a consistent thread context, individual agents can remain stateless, with context passed along as needed. Shared memory or message structures for context sharing can also be implemented if required.

Does this approach make sense, or am I missing any key considerations?

discussion_title: How the protocol intends to handle user thread handoff mechanism ?
author: fernando-torres-blip-ai
raw_text:
Hi @ognis1205, 

Thank you for the reply !
Yes, I belive thats the path to achieve this behaviour.

Following this approach, I'm wondering how to implement this event from remote agent to the orchestrator actor, saying that a handoff must be performed. Do you believe there should be a protocol core object to represent this message?

discussion_title: How the protocol intends to handle user thread handoff mechanism ?
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Hey @fernando-torres-blip-ai,

There are actually two viable approaches depending on how much control you want the orchestrator (client) to maintain during task execution:

**Passive Handoff (`Artifact`-based decision)**:
The orchestrator can wait for each agent to return an `Artifact` and decide whether to continue or switch agents based on the artifact content. This requires no additional protocol object — the orchestrator simply interprets the returned artifact as a signal to continue or perform a handoff.

**Active Orchestration (`Message`-driven handoff)**:
Alternatively, the orchestrator can monitor incoming `Message` updates (via `TaskStatus` or `TaskStatusUpdateEvent`) even before an artifact is returned. Based on these intermediate messages, the orchestrator can involve the user or make early handoff decisions. In this case, agents could explicitly send a `Message` with metadata indicating a handoff is recommended. This doesn't require a new core object — just a convention in the existing `Message` or `TaskStatus`.

So to answer your question: I don’t think a new protocol object is strictly necessary — leveraging `Message` or `TaskStatus.message` with a clear metadata convention should be sufficient to express a handoff intent from the agent to the orchestrator.

Please let me know if I’ve overlooked something or misunderstood any aspect of your idea.

discussion_title: How the protocol intends to handle user thread handoff mechanism ?
author: ognis1205
raw_text: The key point is that what you refer to as the "first agent" is actually the orchestrator, and it’s the orchestrator that can determine the appropriate timing for a thread handoff based on the progress of each task. I don’t believe each (chat) agent needs to be responsible for deciding when to hand off. If it seems necessary for each (chat) agent to take on that responsibility, it might be a sign that the design should be reconsidered.

discussion_title: How the protocol intends to handle user thread handoff mechanism ?
author: fernando-torres-blip-ai
raw_text:
Hmm I believe that the Passive Handoff approch is more suitable for what I was imagining.

Im getting the idea by those examples, where each Agent has a tool usually named like `transfer_to_{agent}`
[OpenAI Agents SDK - Handoffs](https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/handoffs/)
[ADK - Agent Transfer](https://google.github.io/adk-docs/agents/multi-agents/#b-llm-driven-delegation-agent-transfer)
[Langgraph swarm - handoff tool](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph-swarm-py?tab=readme-ov-file#customizing-handoff-tools)

This paradigm allows more complex agent networks without the need of a orchestration agent intercepting every chat interaction.

You're idea of an artifact based implementation seems great ! 

But I believe that to really reach a full remote multi agent system, where different companies build their agents and connect to the system using A2A. It seems interesting that the schema of this artifact should be shared at the protocol spec, what do you think?

discussion_title: How the protocol intends to handle user thread handoff mechanism ?
author: ognis1205
raw_text: I did a quick investigation into the API of the tool you mentioned as an example. IIUC, I believe these are essentially defined as the edge of the A2A network. In other words, they are tools that should be defined on the Client side when constructing an A2A network. Therefore, from the perspective of the current A2A protocol design philosophy, I think we need to solidify the specification for Agent Discovery a bit more before deciding whether an object is necessary. Alternatively, we might need to consider the Agent Discovery specification itself with this in mind. Based on the current specification, I believe a similar mechanism could be constructed using the Message-driven Handoff mentioned above, but when it comes to defining a dedicated schema for Agent Handoff and/or Agent Network Formation (Discovery), we may want to approach that decision more carefully.

discussion_title: Why Aren't Tasks Tied to Skills?
author: patwhite
raw_text:
As the spec is written, you have skills and tasks, but no connection between the two of them. Related, there's no notion of a task catalog (or really, any structure around tasks at all). This is a bit worrisome in the broader context - LLMs are very hard to guardrail for companies operating at the scale who would be writing A2A servers (ie NOT the OpenAI's of the world), so what stops anyone from just putting very simple prompt breaking and burning agent resources to perform arbitrary actions? If feels like the skills should literally be a catalog of tasks you can call, but maybe I'm missing something? 

I'm implementing support for this in https://github.com/Traego/scaled-mcp and want to make sure I'm fully understanding the data model before I start coding.

It is recommended that agents authorize on a per-skill basis (for example, OAuthScope 'foo-read-only' could limit access only to 'generateRecipe' skills).

That doesn't negate the security risk, it just shifts it. I'm building out an AI Gateway and it won't solve the problem I'm outlining. The way the spec is written, you're basically just exposing agents to the world and hope that users respect the skills and don't try to abuse the agents.

Put another way - even behind a gateway, if there's any aspect where user input is reaching the agents themselves, they can be abused unless there's a lot of guardrails. author: patwhite
raw_text: Yes - basically, as the protocol exists today, it’s a kinda watered down chat protocol. As it is your very first agent always has to do validation then honestly probably convert the initial request to a well known json object to hand down to the rest of the agents - why not just skip your middle man and have tasks take a pre defined input

discussion_title: Why Aren't Tasks Tied to Skills?
author: hamidra
raw_text:
Based on my understanding of the A2A protocol specification, the protocol serves as an interface to an agent, allowing other agents send prompts to an Agent, similar to a chatbot or other UIs which allows human prompt the agents. Skills in this context act more like hints to client agents about the types of services the server they can ask the agent to provide. I don’t believe explicitly linking tasks to skills is necessary or directly addresses the concerns you’ve raised, as tasks are technically just some unstructured prompts submitted to the agent.

If the server agent is properly guardrailed, it should evaluate incoming task prompts and respond appropriately, for example, rejecting tasks it cannot or should not perform with a message like, “Sorry, I cannot perform this task”. Your concern about bad actors exhausting agent resources is valid, but mapping tasks to a predefined catalog of skills doesn't seem to mitigate this risk. A bad actor could still submit resource intensive prompts or even unrelated prompts under a supported skill, bypassing any such mapping.

A robust protection must be implemented by the guardrails themselves, which should be designed to evaluate task prompts for malicious intent, or attempts to break the system.

discussion_title: Cloud native agent platform on Kubernetes
author: fapont
raw_text:
Hello there, thank you for defining this new protocol ! 🤗

I see a parallel between the A2A protocol and [Kserve's Open Inference Protocol](https://kserve.github.io/website/master/modelserving/data_plane/v2_protocol/) which enable abstractions like `InferenceService` which totally abstract the underlying ML framework or inference server. I strongly believe we could do the same with this protocol by defining an `A2AServer` as a Custom Resource Definition to standardize agent deployment.

Random ideas:
- **Framework Compatibility**: Various agent libraries (CrewAI, Google ADK, PydanticAI) could implement A2A-compliant servers, or developers could wrap their code in an API similar to the [A2A examples](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/main/samples/python/common/server/server.py).
- **Agent Abstraction**: A2A servers could abstract two types of agents:
  - Simple agents (LLM + Tools + Prompt)
  - Complex agents (represented as interaction graphs, like those built with CrewAI or LangGraph)
- **Gateway Exposure**: A2A servers running in the cluster could be exposed behind a Gateway with an automatically populated `.well-known/agent.json` based on deployed and ready agents.
- **Built-in Registry**: Utilize etcd as an out-of-the-box agent registry since A2A servers would be represented as Kubernetes resources.
- **Kubernetes Ecosystem Benefits (the most important)**: Take advantage of Kubernetes features for reliability and efficiency:
  - Multiple agent replicas
  - Scale-to-zero capabilities
  - Service mesh integration (mTLS, advanced traffic management, ...)
  - Advanced deployment strategy (crucial since small prompt changes can drastically alter agent behavior)
  - ...

I would be greatly honored if these ideas resonated with you and if you would like to discuss them further. Please feel free to share your thoughts if you believe this project deserves to be developed. 😊

Everything is not crystal clear in my head, which is why I started developing a proof of concept of such a system to help me clarify everything.

**Note**: I've heard about `kagent` which is designed to build agents on Kubernetes, but I believe that we should focus our discussion about "How to standardize the way we deploy agents on Kubernetes?", since many frameworks exist to build agent and the A2A protocol defines standards to make them all "discuss" together.

In the agent card, we need to describing an agent's capabilities, skills. https://google.github.io/A2A/#/documentation?id=agent-card

So if I understand correctly: 

agent = underline LLM + tools.

Why not just make all tools available to the underline LLM?  So one single LLM has all the capabilities. (As LLM is trained using all human knowledge so it is general and should be able to understand any tool)

discussion_title: Why individual agents with a subset of skills? Why not a single LLM accessing to all skills like MCP?
author: bobtista
raw_text: I think you mean underlying LLM. The reason we make individual agents with a subset of skills is that we are limited by the context window. Each tool/skill takes up some of that, and an individual agent will struggle to use more than a handful of tools at this point. Try adding 50 tools to an agent using an mcp client eg Claude Desktop, it won't work.

discussion_title: Why individual agents with a subset of skills? Why not a single LLM accessing to all skills like MCP?
author: kyleacmooney
raw_text:
Adding tools increases context and can degrade tool-selection accuracy.  Here is a good article about this: https://blog.langchain.dev/react-agent-benchmarking/

Tangentially, I know Cursor for example was telling users that 40 is the limit for MCP tools.

discussion_title: Why individual agents with a subset of skills? Why not a single LLM accessing to all skills like MCP?
author: wjcwjc77
raw_text:
Imagine this: modern smartphones host a multitude of apps, each designed to perform specific tasks rather than having a single app handle everything. In the future, the capabilities of agents might evolve in a similar fashion.

An agent's performance fundamentally depends on the power of its underlying LLM, yet different models specialize in distinct domains—the most straightforward example being the contrast between language models and vision models.

discussion_title: Why individual agents with a subset of skills? Why not a single LLM accessing to all skills like MCP?
author: nikhilmaddirala
raw_text:
> In the future, the capabilities of agents might evolve in a similar fashion.

Maybe. But until we reach this potential future state, the best practice as of today is to limit the number of tools each agent can use.

discussion_title: Why individual agents with a subset of skills? Why not a single LLM accessing to all skills like MCP?
author: vishalmysore
raw_text: Agree this is really a good design , I believe a key reason for skill segmentation in A2A is that each agent is designed to run on different machines, each with its own capabilities, constraints, and responsibilities. Centralizing all tools under a single LLM would not only create a single point of failure but also reduce flexibility and scalability. Distributed agents allow for parallelism, fault tolerance, and more secure isolation of tasks. Much like we don’t expect a banking app to handle grocery shopping, agents in A2A are intentionally specialized to ensure modular, maintainable, and resilient architectures—where each agent excels at its defined role while collaborating with others to accomplish broader goals.

discussion_title: Why individual agents with a subset of skills? Why not a single LLM accessing to all skills like MCP?
author: dayuyang1999
raw_text:
I think this may be a critical reason. So basically we (human) determinstically define the role of each agent and only allow agent to access a subset of tools.  It is intuitive the selection of tools will be more accurate since the search/action space goes smaller.

I believe this is a low-hanging fruit to improve LLM agents' capaibility in a specific task. The con is obviously the limited scalability since human is invovled in the scaffold design.

However, in the future, when LLM have stronger long-context and retrieve capability. So we will no longer need to define specialized agent at that time?

You, as the service provider, expose one agent to the customers, but such an agent needs to collaborate with other agents (on other machines) to finish a task. In this case, not all skills are equipped by one single agent.

Excited to share a project I've been working on: a comprehensive documentation website for the **Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol**.

discussion_title: A general database containing agent cards
author: bigmac7
raw_text: I was thinking about a general database which contains remote agent addresses - something like hugging face - so agents can reference this database when handing off tasks - is this in the works?

discussion_title: A general database containing agent cards
author: alan-silverstreams
raw_text:
Been working on something in this direction — [ClawNexus](https://github.com/SilverstreamsAI/ClawNexus) maintains a local registry of discovered agents and can generate A2A Agent Cards for each.

Right now it's focused on LAN discovery (mDNS, UDP broadcast, HTTP probing) for OpenClaw instances, but the registry side (`/a2a/cards` endpoint) returns all known agents as Agent Cards. There's also a public registry component for cross-network discovery.

The A2A spec mentions "Curated Registries" as a discovery strategy but leaves the API undefined — so there's definitely room for something like what you're describing.

discussion_title: Python SDK: https://github.com/pcingola/a2a_min
author: pcingola
raw_text:
Here is a simple "echo A2A" example code in a few lines:

```
from a2a_min.abstraction import AgentAdapter, A2aMinServer, AgentInvocationResult

class EchoAgent(AgentAdapter):
    """ A simple echo agent that repeats the user's message """
    def invoke(self, query: str, session_id: str) -> AgentInvocationResult:
        """Echo back the user's query."""
        return AgentInvocationResult.agent_msg(f"Echo: {query}")

# Start the echo agent server
A2aMinServer.from_agent(EchoAgent()).start()
```

raw_text:
As stated below:

https://google.github.io/A2A/#/documentation?id=agent-card

Currently, I understand the specification maps a single agent to a single host (base URL). However, there may be cases where a host serves as a routing environment for multiple agents, mapping them to different paths under the same base URL.
Do you plan to incorporate such a use case into the specification in the future?For example, something like:

`https://<BASE_URL>/.well-known/agents.json`

 could return a list of `AgentCard`s, where each entry includes the `path` for the corresponding agent.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: kthota-g
raw_text:
Thanks for starting the thread. Currently if you like more than one agent to be available for open discovery on the same host url, the agent card should be available at a different path per agent or they have to be available via a different port. If don't require open discovery for the agent, you can expose the agent card at any url and have the A2AClient read from the custom url.

As documented  in [Agent Discovery](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/topics/agent_discovery) topic, we think enterprises scenarios may require an agent to fetch a list of entitled agents via a registry.

We are looking to provide more clarity on this requirement in the next revision of the spec. If you have any recommendations or suggestions, please feel free to share with us.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: vishalmysore
raw_text:
To support multiple agent groups within the existing AgentCard schema, I would recommend introducing an optional agentGroups property. This would allow each agent to be logically categorized under one or more groups, enabling more flexible organization, routing, and management of agents in multi-agent systems. The modification can be implemented as follows:

```
"agentGroups": {
  "type": "array",
  "title": "Agent Groups",
  "items": {
    "type": "string"
  },
  "default": [],
  "description": "Logical grouping(s) the agent belongs to, used for scoping, routing, or permissioning"
}
```
This field should be added to the properties section of the AgentCard schema. It does not need to be required, allowing backward compatibility with existing definitions. With this enhancement, the platform will be better equipped to support use cases involving agent clusters, federation, or role-based agent behaviors. 

For example in my java implementation of A2A I am fitting 2 different types of agents using agent groups if would be good to get this support out of the box in a2a.json

https://github.com/vishalmysore/a2ajava/tree/main/src/main/java/io/github/vishalmysore/example

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: ognis1205

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
As an alternative option, I propose moving properties associated with the `AgentCard` API call (such as authentication and provider information), which are common to all agents under the same `BASE_URL`, into the `AgentIndex`. Alternatively, we could return a list of `AgentCard` objects within the `agents` field of the `AgentIndex`, with common properties being moved to the `AgentIndex` itself. This would simplify the structure and avoid redundancy. Additionally, I suggest not adopting the sub-resource pattern for returning metadata for each individual agent.

However, one potential downside of these approach is that it could lead to a more complex `AgentIndex` structure if too many properties are centralized there. Balancing the scope of `AgentIndex` with the need for clear, maintainable separation of concerns will be crucial.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: ognis1205
raw_text: While introducing a separate schema does add to the number of entities being managed, I think it may be a preferable direction—especially from a discovery perspective, where such a schema is necessary regardless of whether or not an external registry is used. Rather than overloading the `AgentCard`, it would be ideal to introduce a separate schema (such as `AgentIndex`) and organize the necessary properties based on their distinct concerns.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: darrelmiller
raw_text:
FYI, .well-known does not allow path segments before the .well-known segment  https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5785#section-3

So, if .well-known is to be used then it really needs to be a list of agents in the file, or you could leverage the "very nearly published" api-catalog well-known endpoint to point to agent cards. https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-httpapi-api-catalog-08.html

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL

I think it makes sense to describe each agent endpoint using a `linkset`, following the `api-catalog` format (although it's still a draft and we cannot find any concrete schema/format yet). For the `AgentCard`, it seems reasonable to expose it as a subresource retrievable via a `GET` request from the endpoint specified in the `linkset`.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: darrelmiller
raw_text:
> Is this a valid and interoperable way to represent a custom boolean flag using extension attributes in a link target object? Appreciate any feedback or clarification on this usage!

As is mentioned in the linkset RFC, there is no guarantee of interoperability.  There needs to be community agreement around these extension target attributes.  It is certainly something could be coordinated through a registry or specification of extensions.  As the RFC requires the values to be a string, the fact that "true" looks like a boolean is arguably a coincidence.  All that matters is that consumers of the extension agree on a sequence of characters and its meaning.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
## PoC: SDK Example for Agent Mounting and API Catalog Discovery

I’ve implemented a proof-of-concept SDK that demonstrates how to define and mount multiple A2A agents using `A2AStarletteRouteBuilder` and `A2AStarletteBuilder`.  Each agent is configured with its own `AgentCard`, skill set, and handler, and mounted to a shared Starlette application. The resulting server responds with a valid A2A catalog at `/.well-known/api-catalog.json`, like the following:

```json
{
  "linkset": [
    {
      "anchor": "http://localhost:9999/a2a/hello/",
      "describedby": [
        {
          "href": "http://localhost:9999/a2a/hello/agent.json",
          "type": "application/json"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "anchor": "http://localhost:9999/a2a/echo/",
      "describedby": [
        {
          "href": "http://localhost:9999/a2a/echo/agent.json",
          "type": "application/json"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

### Minimal Example Code

```python
@click.command()
@click.option('--host', default='localhost')
@click.option('--port', default=9999)
def main(host: str, port: int):
    hello_skill = AgentSkill(
        id='hello_world',
        name='Returns hello world',
        description='just returns hello world',
        tags=['hello world'],
        examples=['hi', 'hello world'],
    )
    hello_card = AgentCard(
        name='Hello World Agent',
        description='Just a hello world agent',
        url=f'http://{host}:{port}/a2a/hello',
        version='1.0.0',
        defaultInputModes=['text'],
        defaultOutputModes=['text'],
        capabilities=AgentCapabilities(streaming=True),
        skills=[hello_skill],
        supportsAuthenticatedExtendedCard=False,
    )
    hello_handler = DefaultRequestHandler(
        agent_executor=HelloWorldAgentExecutor(),
        task_store=InMemoryTaskStore(),
    )
    hello_agent = A2AStarletteRouteBuilder(
        agent_card=hello_card,
        http_handler=hello_handler,
    )

    echo_skill = AgentSkill(
        id="echo",
        name="Echo input",
        description="Returns the input text as is",
        tags=["echo"],
        examples=["Hello!", "Repeat after me"],
    )
    echo_card = AgentCard(
        name="Echo Agent",
        description="An agent that echoes back your input.",
        url=f"http://{host}:{port}/a2a/echo",
        version="1.0.0",
        defaultInputModes=["text"],
        defaultOutputModes=["text"],
        capabilities=AgentCapabilities(streaming=True),
        skills=[echo_skill],
        supportsAuthenticatedExtendedCard=False,
    )
    echo_handler = DefaultRequestHandler(
        agent_executor=EchoAgentExecutor(),
        task_store=InMemoryTaskStore(),
    )
    echo_agent = A2AStarletteRouteBuilder(
        agent_card=echo_card,
        http_handler=echo_handler,
    )

    server = (
        A2AStarletteBuilder()
            .mount(hello_agent)
            .mount(echo_agent)
            .build()
    )
    uvicorn.run(server, host=host, port=port)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        main()
    except Exception:
        print(traceback.format_exc(), file=sys.stderr)
```

This can serve as a practical SDK example when reviewing the following PR:

- [google-a2a/A2A#642](https://github.com/google-a2a/A2A/pull/642) – API catalog spec update (this is the primary PR to review)

The following is the related PoC implementation of the SDK:

- [google-a2a/a2a-python#109](https://github.com/google-a2a/a2a-python/pull/109) – SDK implementation

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: kthota-g
raw_text:
In the current implementation, each A2A server publishes it's own Agent Card describing it's capabilities. A standalone A2AServer may not know the presence of other servers or agent cards and so they won't be publishers of such catalog.

For example: Let's assume an enterprise that has two agents: 1) IT Agent, 2) HR Agent. Each agent may be hosted separately, offers different set of capabilities and each publish their own agent card.

These published agent cards could be part of an Agent catalog hosted by another service (let's call it registry). 

If there is an orchestrator agent that can call these agents, it can be a consumer of the Agent Catalog to discover these agents and then use the provided Agent Card to invoke the agents. The Agent catalog can have entitlement mechanism to return the list of agents available for a given consumer.

There is usefulness for Agent Catalog, but we think exposing an agent catalog is not the responsibility of an A2AServer, it should rather be available via a separate service that enables agent discovery.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Thank you for sharing your thoughts @kthota-g — I really appreciate the clarity and context you’ve provided.

I completely agree with the point that the Agent Catalog / Agent Registry should be implemented as a separate service (and I believe this view is shared by others participating in this discussion as well).

What we are primarily discussing here, I believe, is how to support Open Discovery via the `.well-known` URL in cases where multiple agents are hosted under a single host or base URL.

The current specification and documentation assume a "one host, one agent" model, where the `.well-known/agent.json` endpoint is used. Also, as per the specification, the `.well-known` URL cannot be placed under a path segment (i.e., it must reside at the root of the origin).

Given this, my understanding is that when multiple agents are hosted under a single base URL, agent discovery via `.well-known` becomes infeasible, and therefore using an external Agent Registry / Agent Catalog is the appropriate approach.

Just to clarify — this is not meant as a strong objection or disagreement, but rather an attempt to confirm our mutual understanding. Would you agree with this interpretation?

P.S.,
Also, I think it’s a clean and reasonable design choice not to bring the API catalog itself into the A2A specification, since it's a separate concern with its own scope.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: kthota-g
raw_text:
In agreement with your comment.

An agent can leverage well-known url for publishing it's agent card. This enables an easy discovery by systems.

Agents can choose to expose their agent cards at custom urls and facilitate the discovery of these agents via a catalog/registry kind of service. Such registries can be scoped within enterprise boundaries for facilitating the discovery and entitlements.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: sfitts
raw_text:
Regarding this...

> An agent can leverage well-known url for publishing it's agent card. This enables an easy discovery by systems.

This works if and only if the agent is the only one available from a given root URI.  As soon as you want to have 2 (or more) agents hosted by the same A2A server, this no longer works.

Is this truly the intent of the specification?  If so, what is the reasoning behind it.  IMO it seems overly restrictive and means that no system of any size can use the simple discovery mechanism.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
# Thoughts on the Use of `.well-known` in A2A Protocol Design

This summarizes some considerations and suggestions regarding the use of `.well-known` URIs in the context of the A2A protocol. It is shared in the spirit of constructive discussion and future-proof protocol design.

---

## `.well-known` Suggests an Intent Toward Standardization

- Using or recommending `.well-known` implicitly signals that the protocol or specification may evolve toward broader adoption or standardization.
- As defined in [RFC 8615](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc8615/), the `.well-known` namespace is reserved for interoperable, host-wide metadata and configuration resources.
- Accordingly, any metadata exposed via `.well-known` should be general-purpose and future-proof.

---

## Prioritize Generality and Extensibility

- `.well-known` entries should ideally describe the host's overall protocol support, not a single resource or agent.
- This facilitates extensibility, versioning, and third-party tooling across varied environments.
- OpenID Connect's [`/.well-known/openid-configuration`](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-discovery-1_0.html) serves as a useful precedent: it describes host-level authentication capabilities, not a single user or app.

---

## Be Mindful of Granularity

- A path like `/.well-known/agent.json` that provides only one agent’s information may misalign with the intention of `.well-known` as host-wide metadata.
- Hosts may expose multiple agents, so a design that assumes a single agent per host introduces limitations and ambiguity.

---

## Suggested Structure

- Consider exposing a host-level summary document at `/.well-known/api-catalog.json` (or similar), which:
  - Provides a list of available agents and their capabilities.
  - Points to further metadata (e.g., per-agent `agent.json` files).
  - Aligns with patterns like the [api-catalog Internet-Draft](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-httpapi-api-catalog/08/).

- Individual agent metadata can then live at stable, discoverable paths such as:
  - `/path/to/agent/agent.json`

---

## `.well-known` May Not Always Be Necessary

- If a clean, extensible `.well-known` design is difficult to achieve, it may be more appropriate to adopt external catalogs or registries as the primary discovery mechanism.
- This avoids premature assumptions about host structure or deployment models, while retaining flexibility for future integration.

---

## Consider Separation of Concerns

- A2A protocols often aim to support agent communication, but agent discovery may benefit from being handled by a separate specification layer or registry API.
- Deferring final decisions around `.well-known` structure allows time to better understand real-world use cases and system diversity.

---

## On the Importance of Updating Current Documentation

- Given the current state of documentation, some existing guidance around `.well-known` usage may inadvertently lead to misunderstandings or assumptions about the intended scope and structure of A2A deployments.
- Clarifying the role and recommended usage of `.well-known`—especially in relation to host-level versus agent-level metadata—could help reduce confusion for implementers.
- We therefore recommend revisiting and updating the relevant documentation to reflect a more cautious, future-aware design philosophy.

---

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: cdavernas
raw_text:
> we think exposing an agent catalog is not the responsibility of an A2AServer

@kthota-g I respectfully disagree. The specification has so far taken a highly opinionated stance on how agents should be hosted and executed. In my view, it should not impose a specific architectural model, which is precisely why it should provide a mechanism for documenting multiple agents. Failing to address this issue—and simply stating that it is not the responsibility of an A2A server (without justification)—undermines standardization efforts around discovery and, as a result, harms the specification.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
@kthota-g @cdavernas 

> > we think exposing an agent catalog is not the responsibility of an A2AServer  
>
> @kthota-g I respectfully disagree. The specification has so far taken a highly opinionated stance on how agents should be hosted and executed. In my view, it should not impose a specific architectural model, which is precisely why it should provide a mechanism for documenting multiple agents. Failing to address this issue—and simply stating that it is not the responsibility of an A2A server (without justification)—undermines standardization efforts around discovery and, as a result, harms the specification.

I would like to clarify that the term **"Agent Catalog"** — as proposed — is **not equivalent to a conventional catalog** in the sense that the word might suggest. While I adopted the term "catalog" in reference to the [API Catalog Internet Draft](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-httpapi-api-catalog-08.html), I recognize that this may have led to some misunderstanding.

In particular, the "Agent Catalog" we propose — modeled after the API Catalog draft — differs from conventional catalog services in the following key ways:

| Aspect | IETF-style API Catalog | Conventional API Catalog (e.g., API Catalog Service) |
|--------|-------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Self-published, machine-readable enumeration of APIs/agents | Centralized discovery and governance of many APIs |
| **Ownership** | Published by the server (API/agent provider) | Potentially operated by third-party platforms |
| **Access Method** | Served via `/.well-known/api-catalog` in JSON | Often via platform-hosted web portals or APIs |
| **Format** | Follows [RFC 9264](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9264/) (`application/linkset+json`) | Platform-specific formats |

This Agent Catalog is conceptually aligned with *self-hosted discovery*, not centralized indexing.

discussion_title: Support for Hosting Multiple Agents under a Single Base URL
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Hi @liuming-dev , thanks for the example — it helps clarify the use case!

Just a quick note regarding the `.well-known` path:

https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/discussions/166#discussioncomment-13188099

So unfortunately, URLs like `https://www.example/xxx/agent1/.well-known/agent.json` are not valid under the `.well-known` convention.

If your goal is to host multiple agents under the same domain and still support some form of open discovery, there are a few possible approaches:

 - [API Catalog](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9727/)
 - [ORD](https://open-resource-discovery.github.io/specification/)
 - Other similar specifications and/or formats
 
 If Open Discovery is not strictly required for your setup (e.g. you control the client), then it may be more pragmatic to:

 - Hardcode the endpoints per agent in the client app, or
 - Use a registry-like service that maps logical agent names to endpoint URLs.

Let me know if you’d like help sketching one of these approaches in more detail!

My exploration using `AgentSkill` was an attempt to self-critically examine whether similar functionality could be achieved within the currently recommended approach. I share the same concerns as you — particularly when it comes to skill routing within a single agent.

While I still believe that `.well-known` should expose more general and comprehensive metadata, in the context of the "one-host, one-agent" model, we might consider mechanisms to narrow down skill resolution using additional information like tags when sending messages to a remote agent.

author: gregboero

discussion_title: Could / Should  A2A evolve toward (transport and message-format)-agnostic communication?
author: robomotic
raw_text: Very good idea, I think the main problem here is then make sure the off-the-band authentication mechanism works with the corresponding protocols for example how would you do the OAuth on the remote socket?

discussion_title: Could / Should  A2A evolve toward (transport and message-format)-agnostic communication?
author: gregboero
raw_text:
Thanks! Great point , authentication is definitely a key consideration here.

In my view, the responsibility for authentication would naturally shift to the underlying transport protocol, which often has built-in mechanisms to handle it securely and efficiently.

For example:  in the "protocols" section for socket communication i used RSocket, cause by default it allows embedding authentication metadata (e.g., via MIME types like message/x.rsocket.authentication.bearer.v0) during the initial connection setup, enabling bearer tokens, JWTs, or custom auth schemes as part of the connection handshake.

As for documenting the authentication mechanisms, I think the existing AgentAuthentication block could be extended to reflect protocol-specific configurations, if needed . Though ideally, the authentication approach could be unified across protocols unless there's a strong reason not to.

discussion_title: Could / Should  A2A evolve toward (transport and message-format)-agnostic communication?
author: danja
raw_text:
A very good question. I've been around long enough to remember XML-RPC and SOAP which attempted to reinvent local computing on the Web. The fundamental flaw is that RPC is a completely different paradigm to REST (by Fielding's definition, not the way it's usually meant these days). Interop will always be handicapped by impedance mismatch.

But viewed from a different angle, I don't think the JSON-RPC focus is an obstacle. I've not played yet, but going forward I think it may be useful to consider it as a discovery/first-contact layer. It clearly supports a lot of stuff - for which it was designed. When mismatches become problematic (auth protocols have been mentioned, I suspect there'll issues with some standard web bits, and then there's the whole messaging world, IoT etc), I reckon it should be viable to use content negotiation variants and upgrade the protocol on the fly as required. On the agents' cards is a declaration of the protocols they understand. 

By analogy - if a Moroccan, first language Arabic and an Italian (Italian!) meet, they might greet in English, use that to determine that they are both fluent in French, flip to that for conversation.

(btw, I've spent a lot of time around the general problem because I've been working on a protocol in the same space as A2A, codenamed "Lingue", from a plural of "lingua franca", which is a big hint on how I've been approaching it. Right now I'll have to change track a little, look at interop with A2A. Seeing JSON-RPC brought a bit of sick into my mouth...but net-net I believe A2A will enable a big step forward. *Any* common protocol provides a foothold into the network effect.)

discussion_title: Sign agent cards for the love of god!
author: robomotic
raw_text:
The assumption so far is that the protocol will assume the owner of the agent belongs to the owner of the domain FQDN where is deployed but there are a number of cases where this will be violated for example a hosting provider may not allow the user to write the agent card or the application logic is in another domain and the card on another subdomain.
In terms of security a malicious threat actor may even overwrite the agent card to point to its malicious services.
The solution is quite simple and will be to include a field for digitally signing the card with a GPG/PGP key, in that way you can figure out which organization/person owns the card.
Cheers!

We could also try to integrate signing and verification directly in the library, to make it transparent for the users.

There are 2 orthogonal axes here:

- the signature format: I will +1 to use DSSE as that it simple to use and contents can also be inspected with `jq`
- the signing material: keys, certificates, Sigstore. I'd suggest Sigstore as that removes the need for the agent providers to maintain keys, can allow you to monitor the transparency log to see what agents are released with your identity (contrasting with signing with key/certificate when if the signing key/cert gets leaked you have no way to determine what other objects have been signed with that and can only revoke the key/certificate and issue new ones, rotating).

discussion_title: Sign agent cards for the love of god!
author: mikeas1
raw_text:
Alright so a month later and I've had time to try to work up a proposal.

I had a few thoughts based on the discussions here and elsewhere:

1. Signatures directly on the AgentCard (rather than out-of-band) provide a much more direct way for users to understand how to find and verify signatures. Out-of-band signatures are still possible, of course -- I can't stop you from doing it!
2. Signatures should be optional, and adding them should be backwards compatible. This means we don't fundamentally change what is returned from an AgentCard endpoint -- not directly serving an envelope-wrapped AgentCard.
3. The format should be familiar (meaning: not novel, leveraging existing standards) and lightweight.
4. We should be able to leverage existing libraries in every major language to assist in the verification process
5. Adopting signatures should be simple and not require major new infrastructure/systems buy in. But leveraging systems like sigstore should be possible.

Those desires led me to a pretty straightforward option: [JSON Web Signatures (JWS)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7515).

So, here's the proposal. Please let me know if you believe this would solve what you're looking for with signed agent cards.

# **A2A: Agent Card Signing**

## *mikeas@ / Draft: 2025-07-14*

## *Public* 

# **Overview**

We propose adding signatures to the AgentCard to allow verifying the integrity of the AgentCard content. This is an optional feature of AgentCards. We will use the standard JSON Web Signatures (JWS) format for representing the signature. Trust in the signature can be established via standard JWS fields that indicate the source of keys used to create the signature.

# **AgentCard Signature**

We will extend the AgentCard to include a `signatures` field. This field will store a list of JWS JSON objects with detached payload. This adheres to the [RFC 7515 specification](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7515#section-3.2) for JSON Web Signatures, and [RFC 7797 specification](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7797#section-5.3) for detached payloads. The following is the approximate typescript definition of the signature type:

```ts
interface JSONWebSignature {
  // The JWS protected header. A base-64 encoded JSON object containing JWS header values. This value is included
  // in the signing payload, therefore it is protected from alteration.
  protected: string;
  // The base64-encoded signature, computed according to the algorithm specified in the JWS header.
  signature: string;
  // The unprotected JWS header. This contains information for applications interpreting the signature, but
  // is not included in the signature and therefore can be tampered with.
  header?: { [string]: string };
}
```

**Alternative:** We could collapse the signature down to a single string by using the compact serialization format with a detached payload. All header values would need to be placed in the protected header for this to work. The resulting signature is a string of the form `<b64-protected-header>..<b64-signature>` (note the 2 sequential dots, which is the format for a JWS with a detached payload). This format can be slightly more compact compared to the object version if unprotected headers are not used. I initially did not choose this format since I am biased towards preferring structured formats over string formats, but this format is common and well supported.

A full AgentCard example that includes signatures might look like this:

```ts
{
  "protocolVersion": "0.2.9",
  "name": "GeoSpatial Route Planner Agent",
  "description": "Provides advanced route planning, traffic analysis, and custom map generation services. This agent can calculate optimal routes, estimate travel times considering real-time traffic, and create personalized maps with points of interest.",

  //////// Key addition:
  "signatures": [
    {
      "protected": "eyJhbGciOiJFUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpPU0UiLCJraWQiOiJrZXktMSIsImprdSI6Imh0dHBzOi8vZXhhbXBsZS5jb20vYWdlbnQvandrcy5qc29uIn0",
      "signature": "QFdkNLNszlGj3z3u0YQGt_T9LixY3qtdQpZmsTdDHDe3fXV9y9-B3m2-XgCpzuhiLt8E0tV6HXoZKHv4GtHgKQ"
    }
  ]

```

# **Verification Process**

The signatures contained in the AgentCard are standard JWS format. However, the process for verifying the signature deviates from the norm. This is because the most common version of JWS seen in the wild is presented in the “compact serialization” format. The process I propose here is composed of known standards, and so is not particularly novel. It just stitches them together for our use case.

The primary challenge to be addressed in verifying a signature is **how to create the signature input**. Crucially, how to represent the AgentCard data in a way that both signers and verifiers can reliably construct to come up with an accurate signature. The problem here is that verifying an AgentCard requires 4 steps:

1. Parse the AgentCard JSON  
2. Extract the signatures  
3. Produce the signing input  
4. Verify the signature against the signing input following standard JWS process

The issue is step 3: the signing input includes the payload, but doesn’t include the signature itself, and so we’re faced with an issue where everyone must agree on how to **take their parsed AgentCard data**, perform some transformation, and turn it into the **exact same set of bytes** that act as the payload input. Just serializing to JSON is insufficient: any whitespace differences or field ordering differences would produce different input.

This is a problem that has been addressed before, and there is a standard that provides the answer: [RFC 8785: JSON Canonicalization Scheme (JCS)](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8785). This is a standard specifically developed to solve the problem of producing JSON appropriate for input to cryptographic verification.

So, the process is as follows:

1. Parse the AgentCard JSON  
2. Extract the list of signatures  
3. Produce the signing input:  
   1. Serialize the AgentCard *without the `signatures` field* to JSON, according to JCS  
   2. Base64-URL encode the resulting JSON. This is dictated by the JWS specification.  
4. Verify the signature against the signing input following the standard JWS process

Note that "following the standard JWS process" could mean constructing the [compact serialization form](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7515#section-7.1) (`<protected-header> || '.' || BASE64URL(<agent-card-jcs-json>) || '.' || <b64-signature>`) and using a standard JWS verification library.

Roughly, in python:

```py
# Verify all the signatures on an AgentCard.
# key_locator is a function that retrieves the JWKS necessary to verify the signature.
def verify_agent_card_signatures(card: AgentCard, key_locator):
  signatures = card.signatures
  card_dict = card.model_dump(exclude=['signatures'])
  card_jcs = Canonicalize.canonicalize(card_dict)
  jws_payload = jwcrypto.common.base64_urlencode(card_jcs)
  for sig in signatures:
    jws = jwcrypto.JWS.from_jose_token({'signature': sig.model_dump()})
    jwks = key_locator(jws.jose_header)
    jws.verify(jwks, detached_payload=jws_payload)
    # Likely want to emit a record of this signature being verified, including
    # details like the source of the key. Callers can use that to make policy/trust
    # decisions, such as "agent card must be signed by my-favorite-registry".
    
# A key locator that uses JWS fields to identify the key.
def jws_key_locator(jws_header):
  if jku := jws_header.get('jku'):
    # The JWS header indicates a JWKS URL. Fetch it and use that.
  if jwk := jws_header.get('jwk'):
    # The JWS header directly specifies the JWK.
  if x5u := jws_header.get('x5u'):
    # The JWS header indicates a URL for an x.509 cert. Fetch it and use that.
  if x5c := jws_header.get('x5c'):
    # The JWS header includes an x.509 cert.
  raise UnknownKeyError('cannot determine key to use')
```

## **Optional: Countersigning**

Recommendation: do not include countersigning in the initial version.

One of the limitations of the proposed structure for attaching signatures to the AgentCard is that the process of *countersigning* becomes impossible. Countersigning is the inclusion of a signature as input to another signature. This proves that the countersigner observed that the original signature was present. I’m not entirely clear on the value of countersigning for AgentCards, however I feel it’s important to call out the limitation. Perhaps a scenario where the agent deployment platform (such as Agent Engine) countersigns the AgentCard that’s signed by the agent developer.

The key limitation preventing countersigning is that there is no indication in the JWS of what other signatures would be included in the signing input. This is normally handled via a process of wrapping a full JWS in another JWS, where a JWS becomes the payload to another JWS. This is a simple matter when the JWS embeds the payload to be signed. Our design reverses this and embeds the signature in the payload itself, which prevents wrapping. So, if we want countersigning, we need a means for one signature to indicate that another signature should be included in the signing input. There is no existing standard for this, so we’d be defining a custom extension to JWS to make it work. However, the actual process is relatively simple:

1. A JWS that countersigns a set of other JWS’ indicates it does so via a `refs` field in the protected header. The value of this refs field is the actual `signature` value from the other signature payload.  
   1. Referencing the signature directly is the most direct way of identifying the other signature. Otherwise we must devise a scheme to identify a given signature.  
2. When producing the signing input, the countersigned JWS signatures are left inside the AgentCard `signatures` field before it is serialized to JSON. The ordering of the signatures in the original AgentCard must be maintained.  
   1. Note that only directly referenced signatures need to be included in the AgentCard. Even if the countersigned signature is itself a countersignature, that signature already embeds the originally countersigned data.

**Alternative**: A more standard form of countersigning with JWS is performed via nesting, where the payload to be signed is itself a JWS. We could alter the above process to match this. In essence, the difference is in the production of the signing input: the payload for a countersignature becomes the full compact JWS of the signature to be countersigned. One limitation of this option is that it is not possible to countersign two or more signatures (i.e. signature S3 verifies that it saw both S1 and S2, but S1 and S2 are unrelated).

## **Where do the keys come from?**

To verify the signature, the verifier must be able to retrieve the key used for validating the signature.

A few options are:

1. Leveraging standard JWS fields for indicating keys: [jku, jwk, x5c, x5u](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7515#section-4.1.2).  
2. [Sigstore](https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/overview/) provides infrastructure for publishing certificates usable for verifying signatures, with attestation chains that provide identity and trust. If an AgentCard exposed a [Sigstore bundle](https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/bundle/), verifiers could leverage the Sigstore systems to perform signature verification.  
   1. Sigstore certificates can be used to generate JWS signatures by using the `x5c` field to store the certificate details with no additional work. Linkage to the Sigstore Transparency Log would require a custom JWS header field.  
3. DIDs. DID Documents contain [verification keys](https://www.w3.org/TR/did-1.0/#verification-methods) that can be used for verifying signatures. If an AgentCard contained a DID, verifiers could fetch the DID Document and use it as a source of keys. Signatures could specify a DID URL for the `kid` to indicate the key used for signing.  
4. Out-of-band coordination. It’s entirely possible for two parties to establish a means of communicating a source of keys that does not take place via A2A. This may involve shared knowledge of the meaning of the `kid` field in the JWS header.  
   1. For example, an AgentCard registry could sign all cards it ingests, and users of that registry are expected to know what those keys are (“Every AgentCard in MySecureRegistry is fully signed and verified. Just use the keys published at https://secure-and-fancy.example.com/agent-card-keys.json to check for yourself\!”).

For this initial version of agent card signing, we will go with **option 1**: the `jku, jwk, x5c, x5u` parameters all provide a means for identifying the signing key. AgentCard signers should include one of these fields so that verifiers know which keys to use to verify the signature. Out-of-band coordination is still possible. Which of these parameters to use will depend on the signing situation. We **recommend against** specifying a bare `jwk` with no corresponding link to an identity or chain of trust \-- such a signature provides little to no value. Instead, rely on an x.509 chain of trust or trust in a JWKS URL.

In the future, it may be necessary to add other fields to the AgentCard that can indicate sources of keys (such as DIDs or Sigstore bundles). This should be done carefully, as addition of fields like this is technically backwards compatible for the data object, but may not be  backwards compatible for signature verification.

# **Connection to Trust and Identity** {#connection-to-trust-and-identity}

Including a signature offers one gateway into establishing trust. This is done via the connection between the JWS and the keys used to create the signature. If you can establish a connection between the signing keys and an identity, you can establish a basis for trust. There are a few ways this is accomplished:

1. Via the [`jku` field](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7515#section-4.1.2). This field links to a JSON Web Key Set (JWKS) hosted at a URL. This can be used to establish different facts:  
   1. If there is a means to tie the JWKS referenced to the claimed Provider, **we can verify ownership**  
      1. For example, if the AgentCard claims that it is provided by Google, and the `jku` field references a JWKS URL known to represent Google, we have proved that Google actually did provide this agent.  
   2. If there is a means to tie the JWKS referenced to the Agent itself, **we can verify identity**  
      1. The simple option here is that an agent is served from a given path (`https://agents.example.com/travel/v1`, for example) and the JWKS is hosted on the same domain/path (such as `https://agents.example.com/travel/jwks.json`).  
2. Via the [`x5c`](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7515#section-4.1.6)/[`x5u`](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7515#section-4.1.5) fields. These fields indicate that an x.509 certificate was used to create the signature. This allows both **establishing identity** (asserted via the x.509 certificate) and a **root of trust**, using the same means as is done for TLS today.  
   1. This also offers an alternative path to proof of ownership: if a connection can be made between an identity in the certificate chain and the declared `AgentCard.provider`, we can effectively prove ownership (really what is proved is that a certificate issued by the owner was used to sign the AgentCard).  
3. Via a DID interpretation of the `kid` field. If a DID URL is used in the `kid` field, we can establish that the controller of that DID signed the AgentCard.  
   1. This can prove the DID identity of an agent if:  
      1. The AgentCard also includes a field asserting an identity  
      2. That identity is a DID  
      3. That DID is the one that added the signature  
   2. Example: `”kid”: “did:example:id#kid-1”`

Note that these strategies are *available*, but not required or strictly specified. Tying identity to a JWKS URL is not directly solved by the AgentCard and must be established separately. Similarly, signatures do not fully address the idea of establishing an agent identity and corresponding trust. I discuss this more below.

# **What’s missing/What does this prove?**

This section contains overall thoughts on how signatures fit into the A2A landscape.

Adding signatures to AgentCards is a necessary step for ensuring integrity, but simply having a signature alone is insufficient for proving certain useful properties. On its own, a signature proves only one thing: the content of the AgentCard was not changed from when the holder of the signing key added a signature. What should we take away from this?

Let’s consider a few properties we might want to know about an AgentCard:

1. The AgentCard was not modified from the source it was retrieved from.  
2. The AgentCard was produced by the described agent.  
3. The provider referenced in the AgentCard definitely provides the agent described by the AgentCard.  
4. The AgentCard accurately reflects the capabilities of the agent it references.

Property 1 is the most directly provable with a signature. If you can tie the signing key to the source of the AgentCard (an agent registry, for example), you can prove the AgentCard has not been tampered with. Establishing a link between the source of an AgentCard (such as a URL, or an RPC response from a server) is not specified by the protocol. With enough trust in the source of the AgentCard, this is sufficient evidence for the remaining properties (“I trust that you vetted properties 2-4 and wouldn’t be serving me this AgentCard otherwise”). **This is the primary property targeted for this version of AgentCard signatures.**

Abstractly, properties 2-4 are restatements of the same idea: the AgentCard contains a set of claims (the URL the agent is served from, the skills the agent possesses, and the provider of the agent, respectively), and we’d like to verify those claims somehow. Signatures can help with establishing these properties, however in each case some additional information is required.

We could establish property 2 if we had a means to tie a signing key to the agent URL referenced by the AgentCard. We do not have a direct solution for establishing this property. It is up to verifiers to decide what is sufficient for them. One clear way that agent publishers could opt for is serving their JWKS off of the same path as the agent (I mention this in [Connection to Trust and Identity](#connection-to-trust-and-identity)). Another option is to add protocol-level support for this by adding a protocol RPC for retrieving a key set. Something like: `agent/keys` that returns a JWKS object. Verifiers could retrieve the keys from the `AgentCard.url` by invoking this RPC and using the resulting key set to verify that a signature exists using one of those keys.

To establish property 3 with signatures, we need two pieces of information:

1. Some way to identify the provider.  
2. Some way to tie that identity to a signing key.

There are a few ways of accomplishing this:

* Identify the provider by a domain name, and sign the AgentCard with an x.509 certificate that was issued to that domain.  
* Identify the provider by a JWKS URL, and sign the AgentCard with a key from that JWKS.  
* Identify the provider by a DID, and sign the AgentCard with a verifier key from the DID document.  
* Identify the provider by a claim in an x.509 certificate, and sign the AgentCard with an x.509 certificate that was issued by that provider. This is a model that Sigstore enables.  
* Design a custom protocol for providers that includes a means for communicating signing keys, and have the `provider.url` field in the AgentCard point to an endpoint that implements this protocol.

Property 4 is significantly more nebulous and intersects directly with larger questions of trust and abuse. Assuming we have established that an AgentCard has not been modified (property 1) and that it actually comes from the referenced agent (property 2), determining if an agent can actually perform the skills it claims requires an additional framework for validating agent skills, for which we do not have a direct answer. Some form of trusted credential, where a trusted third-party issues a credential attesting that the agent implements the claimed skill,  or validation framework, where a client has the means to directly test and verify the capability, is required. Stated another way, verifiers require either someone they trust to attest that the agent can perform the skill, or they need a means of proving that themselves. **Establishing the root of trust for skill validation is beyond the scope of A2A**.

In general, the primary property that should be asserted with a signature is integrity. Other properties are possible, but require verifiers to decide on more complicated problems, such as trust frameworks.

# **Out of Band Signing** {#out-of-band-signing}

Out of band signing refers to associating a signature with an AgentCard through a means other than including it directly on the AgentCard. This proposal does not eliminate the possibility of out-of-band signing. However, there’s usually some need to communicate to consumers of an AgentCard that out-of-band signatures are available (unless the verifier wants to just blindly test). We do not currently plan to add any fields to the AgentCard to support this at this time. Instead, if you are interested in out-of-band signing, I would recommend you implement it via Extensions.

# **Relation to Other Protocols**

## **ANS**

[Agent Name Service (ANS)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.10609) is an OWASP protocol that supports secure agent discovery. Agents registering with ANS provide their identity and a public key (as part of a Certificate Signing Request), and successful registration results in an x.509 certificate that is associated with the agent. This certificate asserts the agent’s identity and establishes a chain of trust. Agent Registries return details of registered agents (called an Endpoint), including the agent’s certificate and a registry-created signature verifying the integrity of the agent details. It does not appear that the agent certificate is used to create a signature.

AgentCard signatures are similar to the signatures included on the Endpoint object returned by Agent Registries. An ANS Agent Registry can attach a signature to an AgentCard (if an AgentCard is included in the Endpoint data) using a key controlled by the registry.

## **C2PA**

[C2PA](https://c2pa.org/) provides a framework for asserting content authenticity. It is largely targeted for content provenance, to aid in proving that, e.g., a photo was captured by a device and is unaltered. Included in the protocol is a much more comprehensive provenance and claim verification framework than what is proposed here. C2PA leverages a manifest as the data vehicle for storing claims and signatures, which can be served separately from the content itself. Leveraging C2PA in this way is entirely possible, and matches the idea of [Out of Band Signing](#out-of-band-signing).

AgentCard signatures are intended to be a relatively lightweight option for adding signatures. When applications require the robustness of C2PA, they can use it.

## **Sigstore**

[Sigstore](https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/overview/) provides a way for software developers to include signatures on their published artifacts to prove integrity and authenticity. It does so via a combination of a system for issuing certificates that tie to identities, a transparency log for “witnessing” signatures, and tooling for bringing these pieces together in one package. As mentioned above, sigstore and AgentCard signatures are compatible. Additional work could be done to make use of sigstore simpler, if this becomes a popular choice for producing signatures.

author: mikeas1
