discussion_title: The Java implementation of the a2a - a2a4j, released the first version 0.0.1
author: tomsun28
raw_text:
Hello everyone in the community,

So happy to share here that we have released the first version of the a2a protocol implemented in Java（⚠️Unofficial）.  This version has contributions from 6 contributors.

https://github.com/a2ap/a2a4j/releases/tag/0.0.1   

Feel free to try or give us feedback, have fun!

---

[A2A4J](https://github.com/a2ap/a2a4j) is a comprehensive Java implementation of the Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol, including server, client, examples, and starters. Built on Reactor for reactive programming support, A2A4J enables agents to discover each other's capabilities, collaborate on tasks, and securely exchange information without needing access to each other's internal state.

## 🚀 Features

- ✅ **Complete A2A Protocol Support** - Full implementation of the Agent2Agent specification
- ✅ **JSON-RPC 2.0 Communication** - Standards-based request/response messaging
- ✅ **Server-Sent Events Streaming** - Real-time task updates and streaming responses
- ✅ **Task Lifecycle Management** - Comprehensive task state management and monitoring
- ✅ **Spring Boot Integration** - Easy integration with Spring Boot applications
- ✅ **Reactive Programming Support** - Built on Reactor for scalable, non-blocking operations
- ✅ **Multiple Content Types** - Support for text, files, and structured data exchange
- ✅ **Agent Card Discovery** - Dynamic capability discovery mechanism
- ⚪️ **Push Notification Configuration** - Asynchronous task updates via webhooks
- ⚪️ **Enterprise Security** - Authentication and authorization support

## 📋 Prerequisites

- **Java 17+** - Required for running the application
- **Maven 3.6+** - Build tool

## 🏗️ Project Structure

```
a2a4j/
├── a2a4j-bom/                     # A2A4J dependency management
├── a2a4j-core/                    # Core A2A protocol implementation
├── a2a4j-spring-boot-starter/     # Spring Boot auto-configuration
│   ├── a2a4j-server-spring-boot-starter/   # Server-side starter
│   └── a2a4j-client-spring-boot-starter/   # Client-side starter
├── a2a4j-samples/                 # Example implementations
│   └── server-hello-world/        # Hello World server example
│   └── client-hello-world/        # Hello World client example
├── specification/                 # A2A protocol specification
├── tools/                        # Development tools and configuration
```

## 🚀 Quick Start

### 1. Use A2Aj Build Agent

#### Integrate A2A4j SDK

If you’re building on the `SpringBoot` framework, it is recommended to use `a2a4j-server-spring-boot-starter`.

```xml
<dependency>
    <groupId>io.github.a2ap</groupId>
    <artifactId>a2a4j-server-spring-boot-starter</artifactId>
    <version>0.0.1</version>
</dependency>
```

For other frameworks, it is recommended to use `a2a4j-core`.

```xml
<dependency>
    <groupId>io.github.a2ap</groupId>
    <artifactId>a2a4j-core</artifactId>
    <version>0.0.1</version>
</dependency>
```

#### Expose an External Endpoint

```java
@RestController
public class MyA2AController {

    @Autowired
    private A2AServer a2aServer;
    @Autowired
    private final Dispatcher a2aDispatch;

    @GetMapping(".well-known/agent.json")
    public ResponseEntity<AgentCard> getAgentCard() {
        AgentCard card = a2aServer.getSelfAgentCard();
        return ResponseEntity.ok(card);
    }

    @PostMapping(value = "/a2a/server", produces = MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE)
    public ResponseEntity<JSONRPCResponse> handleA2ARequestTask(@RequestBody JSONRPCRequest request) {
        return ResponseEntity.ok(a2aDispatch.dispatch(request));
    }

    @PostMapping(value = "/a2a/server", produces = MediaType.TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE)
    public Flux<ServerSentEvent<JSONRPCResponse>> handleA2ARequestTaskSubscribe(@RequestBody JSONRPCRequest request) {
        return a2aDispatch.dispatchStream(request).map(event -> ServerSentEvent.<JSONRPCResponse>builder()
                .data(event).event("task-update").build());
    }
}
```

#### Implementing the `AgentExecutor` Interface for Agent Task Execution

```java
@Component
public class MyAgentExecutor implements AgentExecutor {

    @Override
    public Mono<Void> execute(RequestContext context, EventQueue eventQueue) {
        // your agent logic code
        TaskStatusUpdateEvent completedEvent = TaskStatusUpdateEvent.builder()
                .taskId(taskId)
                .contextId(contextId)
                .status(TaskStatus.builder()
                        .state(TaskState.COMPLETED)
                        .timestamp(String.valueOf(Instant.now().toEpochMilli()))
                        .message(createAgentMessage("Task completed successfully! Hi you."))
                        .build())
                .isFinal(true)
                .metadata(Map.of(
                        "executionTime", "3000ms",
                        "artifactsGenerated", 4,
                        "success", true))
                .build();

        eventQueue.enqueueEvent(completedEvent);
        return Mono.empty();
    }
}
```

#### Done

That’s it — these are the main steps. For detailed implementation, please refer to our [Agent Demo example](https://github.com/a2ap/a2a4j/tree/main/a2a4j-samples/server-hello-world).

### 2. Test Run Agent Example

#### Run the Server Hello World

```bash
git clone https://github.com/a2ap/a2a4j.git

cd a2a4j

mvn clean install

cd a2a4j-samples/server-hello-world

mvn spring-boot:run
```

The server will start at `http://localhost:8089`.

#### Get Agent Card
```bash
curl http://localhost:8089/.well-known/agent.json
```

#### Send a Message
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8089/a2a/server \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "method": "message/send",
    "params": {
      "message": {
        "role": "user",
        "parts": [
          {
            "kind": "text",
            "text": "Hello, A2A!"
          }
        ],
        "messageId": "9229e770-767c-417b-a0b0-f0741243c589"
      }
    },
    "id": "1"
  }'
```

#### Stream Messages
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8089/a2a/server \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: text/event-stream" \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "method": "message/stream",
    "params": {
      "message": {
        "role": "user",
        "parts": [
          {
            "kind": "text",
            "text": "Hello, streaming A2A!"
          }
        ],
        "messageId": "9229e770-767c-417b-a0b0-f0741243c589"
      }
    },
    "id": "1"
  }'
```

## 📚 Core Modules

### A2A4J Core (`a2a4j-core`)

The core module provides the fundamental A2A protocol implementation:

- **Models**: Data structures for Agent Cards, Tasks, Messages, and Artifacts
- **Server**: Server-side A2A protocol implementation
- **Client**: Client-side A2A protocol implementation
- **JSON-RPC**: JSON-RPC 2.0 request/response handling
- **Exception Handling**: Comprehensive error management

### Spring Boot Starters

#### Server Starter (`a2a4j-server-spring-boot-starter`)
Auto-configuration for A2A servers with Spring Boot, providing:
- Automatic endpoint configuration
- Agent Card publishing
- Task management
- SSE streaming support

#### Client Starter (`a2a4j-client-spring-boot-starter`)
Auto-configuration for A2A clients with Spring Boot, providing:
- Agent discovery
- HTTP client configuration
- Reactive client support

### Examples (`a2a4j-samples`)

Complete working examples demonstrating A2A4J usage:
- **[Hello World Server](https://github.com/a2ap/a2a4j/tree/main/a2a4j-samples/server-hello-world)**: Basic A2A4j server implementation
- **[Hello World Client](https://github.com/a2ap/a2a4j/tree/main/a2a4j-samples/client-hello-world)**: Basic A2A4j client implementation

discussion_title: How can A2A be combined with LLM? Is it similar to MCP Client being wrapped as Function Calling?
author: lambochen
raw_text:
How can A2A be combined with LLM? Is it similar to MCP Client being wrapped as Function Calling? 

Without restrictions on specific implementation or language, I hope you can share your practices or ideas. Thank you.

discussion_title: How can A2A be combined with LLM? Is it similar to MCP Client being wrapped as Function Calling?
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
(cc: @rajeshvelicheti )

I'm not entirely sure if I understand your question correctly, but broadly speaking:

**Agent = LLM + Prompt + Tool Access + (Memory / State)**

From this perspective, A2A is essentially a protocol or API layer that defines how such agents interact with clients — whether those clients are other services, agents, or LLM-based applications. So when asking how to combine A2A with LLMs, I would interpret that as:

 - Implementing an LLM-backed agent (with tools, context, etc.)
 - Then exposing it via an A2A-compliant API, which could be discovered and invoked by other agents — or even LLMs through mechanisms like Function Calling (which itself can be seen as part of the agent architecture).

In that sense, yes — it's quite similar to how MCP or Function Calling wraps tool-like behavior into structured calls. A2A, however, is a protocol designed for agent-to-agent collaboration, so I think it can be more bidirectional compared to MCP. A2A also adds a layer of discoverability and standardization on top. While the context may differ slightly, this sample might still be helpful:

 - https://github.com/google-a2a/a2a-samples/tree/main/samples/python/agents/a2a_mcp

discussion_title: How can A2A be combined with LLM? Is it similar to MCP Client being wrapped as Function Calling?

It sounds like your suggestion is to give Agent B to Agent A via Function Calling. Is this the recommended usage of A2A? Of course, I can't think of any other more suitable way

If I understand correctly, it’s somewhat like inverting the layers shown in this request/response diagram from the CrewAI + A2A sample (you can ignore the other components — I’m just referring to the layer arrangement.):

Since A2A is a protocol rather than a specific implementation, I assume that using it from within a framework like CrewAI depends on how the community adapts A2A to that framework. discussion_title: A better way to handle Agent stream output for client display
author: KanchiShimono
raw_text:
## Context

I want to return the Agent’s (LLM’s) stream output to the client token by token for users of my chat UI.
From what I understand, the most straightforward way to achieve this using the A2A protocol is to include the tokens in `TaskStatusUpdateEvent.status.message` in response to a `message/stream` request.

However, the current [Python](https://github.com/google-a2a/a2a-python/blob/4767a9515d1a1536035040f5b2cb2f14f3f4b045/src/a2a/server/tasks/task_manager.py#L128-L132) and [TypeScript](https://github.com/google-a2a/a2a-js/blob/a997e4b9ff86412dda78d2c32e372f1dee3cc752/src/server/result_manager.ts#L50-L55) SDK implementations have the following issues:
1. `Task.history` records the history token by token, making it cumbersome to reconstruct conversation history across multiple turns.
2. Each token output triggers access to `TaskStore`, which leads to heavy write load when using a database-backed `TaskStore`.

I believe it’s preferable to store conversation history as a single message per complete Agent response.

## Question

1. Given this, is there a good way to display the Agent’s token output on the client?
2. Has there been any consideration of introducing a new type dedicated to display (e.g., something like `MessageDelta`) that does not update task state?

Given these challenges, we’re looking for guidance on how to achieve seamless performance with A2A in a serverless setup. Is this architecture feasible for agentic workflows, or would a server-based architecture be the more reliable option for managing these workflows?

Any insights or recommendations would be greatly appreciated!

- Is the extension identifier the URI itself? If so, in the SendMessageRequest.Message.extensions, should I list the URIs?
- Are the extension parameters static key-value pairs, or is it a JSON API schema representing a possible request body?

discussion_title: Is there any example for AgentExtension workflow ?
author: darrelmiller
raw_text:
Can you take a look at the examples in the recently updated specification and let us know if these examples make the usage clearer?
https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/#46-extensions

The extension Uri is just a globally unique identifier that can be used as the key in the metadata dictionary.  There is no required for that extension URI to be be dereferenceable.

discussion_title: Message driven A2A
author: c-mysec
raw_text: I was wondering if there are use cases where Event-Driven communication (i.e., Kafka) would be better than HTTP for handling tasks, states, messages and artifacts transport. Ok, currently we have push notifications and SSE. But SSE needs a tunnel initiated by a client agent and is not really 100% async. And push notifications can have delivery and reliability problems,

discussion_title: Message driven A2A
author: c-mysec
raw_text:
For example, when an agent publishes an event (i.e., something has happened), and one **or more other agents** react in a way that the original agent did not anticipate.
To draw a parallel with the HR department: when someone resigns (an event), multiple actions are triggered by different individuals who don't need to be directly prompted. They observe the event and respond according to their responsibilities.
Does this make sense?
I know this can be achieved using traditional event driven (agents listening on currently implemented event topics and streams).
I want to think if there are use cases where event driven A2A is better.

discussion_title: Message driven A2A
author: c-mysec
raw_text: Some advantages of event-driven architecture that A2A can rely on include reliability, built-in retry policies in the infrastructure, and back-pressure handling.

discussion_title: Message driven A2A
author: andreibesleaga
raw_text:
There are already integrations with Kafka (useful for existing Enterprise systems as well).

https://www.confluent.io/blog/google-agent2agent-protocol-needs-kafka/

https://www.kai-waehner.de/blog/2025/05/26/agentic-ai-with-the-agent2agent-protocol-a2a-and-mcp-using-apache-kafka-as-event-broker/

https://www.kai-waehner.de/blog/2025/04/14/how-apache-kafka-and-flink-power-event-driven-agentic-ai-in-real-time/

discussion_title: Message driven A2A
author: c-mysec
raw_text:
A2A JsonRPC messages are messages and can use almost any kind of transport. It doesn't need an "integration".
Of course, you can use an integration but (i think) it adds a useless layer in most cases.
I think the protocol should be transport agnostic. But the standard limits to HTTP, SSE and Push Notification ([3. Transport and Format](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/#3-transport-and-format)).
I want to know which use cases can benefit from using an event-driven transport and why the specs limits the transport.

discussion_title: Message driven A2A
author: dinesh707
raw_text:
# RFC: A2A Protocol Extension for Broker-Based Communication

* Status: Draft / Proposal
* Topic: Asynchronous Transport Layer Extension (Inbox & Registry Pattern)


## 1. Executive Summary
This RFC proposes an extension to the Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol to support Message Brokers as a first-class transport layer. This proposal introduces the Agent Inbox Pattern for communication and a Discovery Registry Topic for stateful agent discovery, moving away from point-to-point HTTP lookups.

## 2. Motivation
In a broker-based architecture, agents shouldn't need to know each other's network addresses.
* **The Inbox Pattern:** Standardizes how messages are routed to specific agents via unique topics.
* **Persistent Discovery:** Traditional A2A requires querying a `.well-known` endpoint. In a broker environment, a persistent "Registry Topic" allows agents to discover the entire ecosystem simply by subscribing to a single stream.

## 3. Agent Discovery & Connection
### 3.1 Current State: Direct HTTP Discovery
Existing A2A relies on direct polling of an `agent.json` file hosted at a specific domain. If the domain is unreachable, the agent is effectively "invisible."

### 3.2 Proposed State: The Registry Topic

We propose a standardized Discovery Channel (e.g., `a2a.registry`).
* **Stateful Stream:** This topic should be configured as a "Compacted Topic" (Kafka) or use "Retained Messages" (MQTT/NATS).
* **Announcement:** When an agent comes online, it publishes its Agent Card to this topic using its `agent_id` as the message key.
* **Continuous Discovery:** Any new agent entering the network can read the `a2a.registry` from the beginning to instantly build a local map of all active agents and their "Inboxes."

#### Updated Agent Card (Inbox Specification)
The Agent Card now explicitly defines its `inbox` for receiving tasks:
```json
{
  "agent_id": "agent-774-beta",
  "name": "ProductionAgent_v1",
  "transports": [
    {
      "type": "broker",
      "provider": "kafka",
      "connection_details": {
        "bootstrap_servers": ["kafka.cluster.local:9092"],
        "inbox_topic": "a2a.inbox.agent-774-beta",
        "registry_topic": "a2a.registry"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

## 4. Technical Specification: The Inbox Pattern
### 4.1 Request-Response Flow

Communication is entirely asynchronous. To maintain the "request-response" semantics of A2A, we utilize the Return Inbox pattern.
1. The Inbox: Every agent listens to their own `inbox_topic`.
2. The Request: The Sender publishes a `task.create` payload to the Receiver's inbox.
3. Return Address: The Sender must include its own inbox name in the message metadata (`reply-to`).
4. The Response: The Receiver processes the task and publishes the status update or result back to the topic specified in the `reply_to` field.

### 4.2 Message Metadata (Headers)
Broker-specific headers must be used to route these messages without parsing the JSON body:

|   Header Key   |             Description             |        Example       |
|:--------------:|:-----------------------------------:|:--------------------:|
| a2a-msg-type   | Type of A2A operation               | task.create          |
| reply-to       | The sender's inbox for the response | a2a.inbox.sender-123 |
| correlation-id | Matches the JSON-RPC ID             | rpc-99821            |
| agent-id       | Identity of the sender              | sender-123           ||

# 5. Comparison Matrix

|   Feature   | HTTP Transport (Current) | gRPC Transport (Current) | Broker Transport (Proposed) |
|:-----------:|:------------------------:|:------------------------:|:---------------------------:|
| Discovery   | HTTP GET /.well-known/   | Registry/Service Mesh    | Subscribe to a2a.registry   |
| Address     | URL (DNS/IP)             | Host:Port                | Inbox Topic Name            |
| Messaging   | POST Request             | RPC Call (Unary/Stream)  | Publish to Inbox            |
| Response    | HTTP Response Body       | RPC Response             | Publish to reply-to Inbox   |
| Persistence | None (Ephemeral)         | None (Ephemeral)         | Broker buffers (Reliable)   |
| Coupling    | Synchronous              | Synchronous/Streaming    | Fully Asynchronous          |

# 6. Backward Compatibility & Interoperability

1. **Registry Bridges:** A bridge service can poll the `a2a.registry` and expose those agents as a dynamic directory for legacy HTTP-only agents.
2. **Inbox Gateways:** A gateway can listen to an Inbox and convert the message into an HTTP POST for agents that cannot connect to the broker natively.

# 7. Security Considerations
* Topic Permissions: The a2a.registry should be "Write-Once/Read-Many" for agent IDs to prevent spoofing.
* Inbox Privacy: Agents should only have "Read" permissions for their own inbox topic and "Write" permissions for others' inboxes.
* Identity: Agent Cards in the registry should be digitally signed (JWS) to ensure the `inbox_topic` actually belongs to the claimed `agent_id`.

discussion_title: Message driven A2A
author: Lancetnik
raw_text: @dinesh707 I have some doubts about the shared topic `a2a.registry`. It could be a bad idea for security reasons. What if I wanted to create a hierarchy of agent groups? In this way, agents from one group should not receive messages from another group. In your design, each agent knows each other.

discussion_title: Message driven A2A
author: Lancetnik
raw_text: Also, it seems to conflict a bit with the current `.well-known` implementation. You should already know the agent's address (or card registry) to retrieve a card. What if we introduced the same RPC API for requesting an agent's card via a known channel? This channel could be owned by the registry or the agent itself.

discussion_title: Message driven A2A
author: dinesh707
raw_text:
> @dinesh707 I have some doubts about the shared topic `a2a.registry`. It could be a bad idea for security reasons. What if I wanted to create a hierarchy of agent groups? In this way, agents from one group should not receive messages from another group. In your design, each agent knows each other.

The current A2A Protocol (v1.0-rc) does not have a native "discovery scope" feature. As it stands today, discovery is relatively "flat." It relies on two primary mechanisms that lack built-in hierarchical or group-based scoping.
* The .well-known Pattern: Agents are discovered by querying https://domain.com/.well-known/agent.json. This is purely point-to-point; if you have the URL, you have the agent card.
* Curated Registries: While the specification mentions registries (like "enterprise catalogs"), it leaves the logic for how those registries filter or "scope" agents up to the implementer.

Hence, I'm not sure this is a key worry to have. But at the same time, instead of a single global topic, we introduce the concept of a Namespace. An agent only subscribes to the registry and inboxes within its authorized scope, eg:
* Global Scope: a2a.public.registry (Low-security, public discovery)
* Department Scope: a2a.finance.registry (Only Finance agents)
* Project Scope: a2a.project-x.registry (Ephemeral, high-security)

discussion_title: Message driven A2A
author: dinesh707
raw_text:
> Also, it seems to conflict a bit with the current `.well-known` implementation. You should already know the agent's address (or card registry) to retrieve a card. What if we introduced the same RPC API for requesting an agent's card via a known channel? This channel could be owned by the registry or the agent itself.

**The "Discovery Request" Pattern (RPC via Broker)**
Instead of just a passive stream, we can treat the Registry as a Service. If an agent knows a registry_topic (the "Known Channel") but hasn't seen a specific agent's announcement yet, it can "Query" the registry.

The Request Flow:
* The Requester publishes a discovery.query to the shared a2a.registry topic.
* The Registry Service (or the target Agent itself, if it’s listening) sees the query.
* The Responder sends the full Agent Card back to the Requester’s reply-to inbox.

2. Resolving the .well-known Conflict
To maintain backward compatibility, we can define the Broker as a Virtual Filesystem.
* HTTP Legacy: GET https://domain.com/.well-known/agent.json
* Broker Extension: PUBLISH { "method": "get_card", "agent_id": "..." } TO a2a.registry.rpc

By introducing this, we aren't replacing .well-known; we are providing a transport-agnostic equivalent. If an agent lives entirely inside a Kafka cluster, its "well-known" address is simply the registry topic.

discussion_title: Support part chunk streaming
author: swapydapy
raw_text: # Background A2A protocol currently supports [streaming](https://a2aproject.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#72-messagestream) task updates and result artifacts. Currently the artifacts can be streamed as follows: 1. Multiple artifacts for a task can be sent over multiple [TaskArtifactUpdateEvent](https://a2aproject.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#723-taskartifactupdateevent-object) events. 2. Individual parts for a given artifact can be sent over multiple [TaskArtifactUpdateEvent](https://a2aproject.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#723-taskartifactupdateevent-object) events. But a [Part](https://a2aproject.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#65-part-union-type) needs to be sent as a whole in each TaskArtifactUpdateEvent. The protocol doesn’t allow streaming the chunks of a part over multiple TaskArtifactUpdateEvents. Reason: Since there's no partId to uniquely identify a part, there's no way to identify chunks, belonging to the same-part, across `TaskArtifactUpdateEvent` stream updates. ## Need for Part chunk streaming 1. **TextPart**: A major streaming use case is for large text paragraphs. Within the current paradigm, the text result is one artifact and possibly just **one**[ text-part](https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#651-textpart-object). But this would not allow it to be streamed. Unless the agent breaks the entire paragraph into 2-3 word parts. This is subpar experience, as it will create way too many part objects in the task artifact. 2. **Inline image streaming**: Current FilePart allows for sending images inline as bytes. Currently entire image bytes need to be sent at once. But with streaming, client can progressively render the image. 3. **DataPart**: Currently the user has to wait for the whole dictionary (key,value pairs) to be sent. While streaming keys of a dictionary is tricky and probably specific to how an agent does it, it still allows for quicker feedback for clients. Example for text chunk streaming in current protocol: ``` Lets say I ask agent to write an story: Currently streaming would like: Update 1: Artifact A: Parts = [{text: "There"}] Append = false lastChunk = false Update 2: Artifact A: Parts = [{text: "was"}] Append = true lastChunk = false Update 3: Artifact A: Parts = [{text: "a"}] Append = true lastChunk = false Update 4: Artifact A: Parts = [{text: "jungle"}] Append = true lastChunk = false Update 5: Artifact A: Parts = [{text: "- written"}] Append = true lastChunk = false Update 6: Artifact A: Parts = [{text: "by me"}] Append = true lastChunk = true === Full Artifact on task completion: Artifact A: Parts = [ {text: "There"}, {text: "was"}, {text: "a"}, {text: "jungle"}, {text: "- written"}, {text: "by me"}, ] ``` # Design Introduce 2 new optional fields in [Part](https://a2aproject.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#65-part-union-type) object: 1. `partId` to uniquely identify parts across `TaskArtifactUpdateEvent` stream updates. 1. Help assemble the chunk updates. 2. Also helps bookkeep parts across clients & servers. 2. `lastChunk` to indicate that no more chunk updates will be streamed by the agent. 3. The client can safely assemble the streamed chunks for this partId and process them. 4. The benefit of lastChunk is that the client doesn’t need to wait for the next update event to be streamed. ``` Update 1: Artifact A: Parts = [{partId: "mystory", text: "There"}] Update 2: Artifact A: Parts = [{partId: "mystory", text: "was"}] Append = false lastChunk = false Update 3: Artifact A: Parts = [{partId: "mystory", text: "a"}] Append = true lastChunk = false Update 4: Artifact A: Parts = [{partId: "mystory", text: "jungle", lastChunk: true}] Append = true lastChunk = false Update 5: Artifact A: Parts = [{partId: "credits", text: "- written by me", lastChunk: true}] Append = false lastChunk = true === Full Artifact on task completion: Artifact A: Parts = [ {partId: "mystory", text: "There was a jungle"}, {partId: "credits", text: "- written by me"}, ] ``` ## Backward Compatibility Both `partId` & `lastChunk` fields are optional. If `partId` is not provided, the client can treat each part-chunk as a new unique part. `lastChunk` is optional, since client infer this by: 1. Either the lastChunk is set on the artifact itself. 2. Or a new partID is received in subsequent `TaskArtifactUpdateEvent`.

discussion_title: Support part chunk streaming
author: mikeas1
raw_text:
My overall feeling is that this is just the Artifact wrapper by another name -- we already have the concept of an identifier for an Artifact, and the `Part`s of that Artifact are the chunks. It's not clear to me what this accomplishes more so than using Artifacts differently than you are suggesting. Rather than sticking everything in a single Artifact and identifying different components of it with `partId`s, just use separate Artifacts.

> TextPart: A major streaming use case is for large text paragraphs. Within the current paradigm, the text result is one artifact and possibly just one[ text-part](https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#651-textpart-object). But this would not allow it to be streamed. Unless the agent breaks the entire paragraph into 2-3 word parts. This is subpar experience, as it will create way too many part objects in the task artifact.

I'm not so sure this is actually a subpar experience. Developers can choose the level of chunking they provide on the TextPart -- it doesn't need to be 2-3 word parts. I expect this will probably come directly from their LLM API of choice, rather than a decision they make directly. LLM APIs have effectively the same design, where one text block is streamed in parts. The primary difference here appears to be whether the resulting Part chunks are concatenated together in the stored Artifact or stored separately, which comes down to overhead of the Part wrapper. I don't think that overhead is so high that it means streaming large text is intractable.

> Inline image streaming: Current FilePart allows for sending images inline as bytes. Currently entire image bytes need to be sent at once. But with streaming, client can progressively render the image.

> DataPart: Currently the user has to wait for the whole dictionary (key,value pairs) to be sent. While streaming keys of a dictionary is tricky and probably specific to how an agent does it, it still allows for quicker feedback for clients.

It seems like both of these can be accomplished with the existing Artifact + Parts. You send multiple DataParts or FileParts with `"append": true`. I'm not sure what the difference would be, other than the concatenation happens server side AND client side, instead of just client side. Clients receiving the stream still need to manage concatenating the chunks identified by the same `partId` together.


That said, I do think that there are some outstanding questions on how best to use Parts with Artifacts. Another interpretation of multiple TextParts in a single Artifact is that they are alternates, perhaps the same text but in different languages. It's not clear to me if that grouping is better than just adding multiple Artifacts, one per language (for example). If we see clear patterns or advantages for adding another layer of hierarchy to Parts, then I think this is a good option. It's a low-cost addition that's backwards compatible, which is ideal.

discussion_title: Support part chunk streaming
author: swapydapy
raw_text:
I say both Artifact, Part have their importance is providing structure to an agent response.

Artifact purpose: A task might ask for multiple things from an agent. An artifact defines one of the resultant object to fulfill the overall goal.

For e.g: Plan a trip to Finland and do the bookings for flight, hotel & activities. In this case, below would be the expected artifacts.
- Artifact A: Trip Itinerary.
- Artifact B: Flight booking.
- Artifact C: Hotel booking.
- Artifact D: First Activity booking.
- Artifact E: Second Activity booking.

Part purpose: They provide semantic structure to the artifact. And allow each part to be contain one logical unit information for that overall artifact.

Eg: Artifact: Flight booking.
- Part A: (TextPart) Summary of the flight, seats type etc.
- Part B: (TextPart) Directions on how to reach airport, how to hotel once your reach.
- Part C: (FilePart) PDF invoice of the flight booking.
- Part D: (DartPart) Ticket PNR, Time of flights, flight No, Seat No.

Treating parts as chunk, and artifacts as wrapper over a result type (text, file or data), has two downsides:
1. We lose this semantic structure of multiple resultant entities for a goal. All entities would need to be broken down into their data type constituents and streamed as artifacts. There will be no API level disctinction.
2. The parts (which are now treated as chunks) are persisted in the datastore as well psued through API. This will cause unnecessary clutter and booking.
3. Even when task is finished, both client & server would have to always assemble these parts to get the final result. The parts being treated as chunks was for streaming, but that chunking would still remain long after the task has been streamed.

Hence, the proposal for part level streaming. Even OpenAI has delta for text part, though a little convulated: https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/responses-streaming/response/output_text/delta

Streaming chunks is a method to send over partial data to the client, so that they don't have to wait for the complete task response. I see three levels benefits of streaming:
1. Client can receive individual artifacts. They don't have to wait for all task artifacts to be sent and they can act on each artifact as they are sent over.
2. Clients can receive individual parts of a specific artifact. This way don't have to wait for entire artifact to be sent and instead can show/process a part, as it is received.
3. (Proposal) Clients can receive chunks for a part, can show realtime sneak-peak of the text/image/data part, without waiting for the whole part.

discussion_title: Support part chunk streaming
author: DracoBlue
raw_text:
I second @mikeas1's opinion here.

The [TaskArtifactUpdateEvent](https://a2aproject.github.io/A2A/v0.2.5/specification/#723-taskartifactupdateevent-object) has 

1. `append` to say if the artifact needs to be replaced or appended with the given parts
2. `lastChunk` to say if it is complete

The [Artifact](https://a2aproject.github.io/A2A/v0.2.5/specification/#67-artifact-object) has: artifactId, optional name and even optional description for everything related to the artifact.

This combination IMHO works for even streaming a pdf artifact or image artifact.

discussion_title: Support part chunk streaming
author: mikeas1
raw_text:
Ok, so breaking it down, I think there are two components to what you're proposing:

1. Adding an additional hierarchy layer to Artifacts, so that they can contain named parts.
2. Ability to stream updates to individual Parts.

I think my primary critique is on the first, but I'm not entirely sure the second is necessary. You can technically do the second without the first, you just identify a Part by its index in the parts array. That's how the OpenAI API does it.

My concern with names on Parts is that it's not clear that it's actually more useful. I'm not clear on why Artifact["Flight booking"].Part["flight summary"] is better than Artifact["Flight Booking/Summary"]. Any organization you make out of named chunks in artifacts can be equivalently created with just artifacts -- you just flatten out the hierarchy.

Giving chunks names just makes Artifacts a 2-level organizational hierarchy (level 1: the containing artifact, level 2: the named chunks within it). That raises the question of why 2 levels is the magic number -- why not 3? Artifact > Component > Chunk? You could say that Artifact["Flight Booking"] > Component["Directions"] > (Chunk["Directions to Airport"](TextPart), Chunk["Map to Airport"](FilePart)) is a better organization. Why not arbitrary? Artifact > [Component > [Component]]?

> Even when task is finished, both client & server would have to always assemble these parts to get the final result. The parts being treated as chunks was for streaming, but that chunking would still remain long after the task has been streamed.

This is true, but I don't know that it's a significant burden. You already need to do this when you're actively listening to the stream. You just do the same thing again when you retrieve it offline.

> Even OpenAI has delta for text part, though a little convulated: https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/responses-streaming/response/output_text/delta

Ok, interesting, so it looks like `response` = `Task`, `output` = `Artifact`, and `Part` ~ `content`. Indeed they can stream content values, though there are some differences: 1. content doesn't have names, it's just addressed by index, and 2. there are separate structures for each type of content, and separate streaming methods for each with semantics specifically tuned to that type.

I guess I'm just not sure that this actually simplifies anything. If you set aside the organization of parts within an artifact, the only functional difference here is whether we concat the `Part`s together at rest or require a client to do it. You still stream content out in effectively the same way, and the client still needs to deal with it.

discussion_title: Support part chunk streaming
author: swapydapy
raw_text:
I think we need to agree on the meaning of each construct: Artifact, Part.

## Artifacts
They denote one complete meaningful resultant entity. Ex:

- So If user asked to create 3 images. Then output would be 3 artifacts, each artifact is an image.
- If user asked to plan a trip. Then each artifact maps to one outcome of that planning:
  - Artifact A: Flight booking
  - Artifact B: Hotel Booking
  - Artifact C: Activity 1 booking
  - Artifact D: Activity 2 booking

## Part
They individual section of the artifact which hold a complete meaning wihtin that artifact. That's why there are differnt types of Part supported: Text, File & Data (Dict). This allows an artifact to compose over multiple heterogenous Parts. Ex: 

Within google drive, user asked to create 3 images. Then there will be 3 artifacts: Each artifact will be one image.

- Part A: File content
- Part B: DataPart: Format, File name, GDrive shareable URL.

Plan a trip:

- Artifact: Flight booking.
  - Part A: (TextPart) Summary of the flight, seats type etc.
  - Part B: (TextPart) Directions on how to reach airport, how to hotel once your reach.
  - Part C: (FilePart) PDF invoice of the flight booking.
  - Part D: (DataPart) Ticket PNR, Time of flights, flight No, Seat No.
 
- Artifact: Hotel booking.
  - Part A: (TextPart) Summary of the hotel booking, room info.
  - Part B: (TextPart) Directions on how to reach hotel.
  - Part C: (FilePart) Voucher hotel booking.
  - Part D: (DartPart) Checkin, Checkout, Breakfast, Lunch timing.

If we flatten out the parts into multiple artifacts, then we are asking the client to be intelligent to infer the grouping from flattened "artifacts". The point of A2A protocol is to provide structure, so that simpler clients can benefit from that `Task` structure. Even if the client is intelligent, the artifact generating agent is much more well-suited to align the parts within one artifact. 
Ex. If there's simple app client for this booking agent. It would be much better if there's one artifact for each of the booking, instead of 3 artifacts just for flight booking: ["Flight/Summary", "Flight/Directions", "Flight/PDF", "Flight/TicketInfo"], ["Hotel/Summary", "Hotel/Voucher", "Hotel/Direction"]

This becomes worse, when there are multiple artifacts. Then all parts would need to be flattened as artifacts to be streamable in current spec.

To your point of that why not go for 3rd level of hierarchy, we are not proposing another level of hierarchy. Just support for part chunks to be streamable, and hence need a unique identifier to tie up the part-chunks. This identifier can be an part-ID, part-name or just simple part-index. From protocol perspective, these chunks are ephemeral and do not exist in synchronous API or the storage layer.

## Why we should not reuse parts for streaming
The protocol dictates "parts" as structure of artifact, so they need to be stored as-is in the protocol synchronous APIs as well as the rest layer. We can not club them together as one part and store it.

If saving as-is, the chunks as parts, would cause blow-up of storage space needed as well as bandwidth for sync calls to getTask, get task history. From a chunk which can be streamed as `string`, will need to be mapped as part structure during storage {kind: "text", text: "some-chunk"}

Even the concatenation computational overhead, is fine for first time streaming, as the agent itself is generating the artifact parts on the fly. But once it has been fully generated, the overhead to concatenate them for each API call is wasteful.

discussion_title: a2a-protocol-validator - Authentication for Agent Card
author: lukehinds
raw_text:
I’m not sure which language you’re using, but in Python, for example, the `AgentCard` is validated using the A2A-type models which has pydantic as its validation mechanism. Personally, I wouldn’t trust that validator - the whole site looks like it was vibecoded together.

If you're concerned about exposing too much of the agent’s capabilities to unauthorized entities through an unprotected `AgentCard` endpoint, but still want the card to be discoverable, you could use the Extended Agent Card instead. This allows you to protect more sensitive information—such as capabilities, skills, etc. - so that it's only visible to users who have been properly authorized.

https://a2aproject.github.io/A2A/specification/#91-fetching-authenticated-extended-agent-card

discussion_title: a2a-protocol-validator - Authentication for Agent Card
author: kthota-g
raw_text:
@rsoika, as @lukehinds  suggested you can validate the agent card json with the types that are shipped part of the python sdk. Here is an example snippet for validating the agent card json.

```
import json

from pathlib import Path

from a2a.types import AgentCard
from pydantic import ValidationError


def validate_agent_card(file_path: str):
    """Validate the AgentCard JSON file against the Pydantic model."""
    print(f"--- Attempting to validate file: {file_path} ---")
    try:
        with Path(file_path).open('r') as f:
            data = json.load(f)

        # Create an instance of the AgentCard model for validation
        agent_card = AgentCard(**data)

        print("\n✅ Validation Successful!")
        print("Pydantic Model Object:")
        print(agent_card.model_dump_json(indent=4))

    except FileNotFoundError:
        print(f"\n❌ Error: The file '{file_path}' was not found.")
    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
        print(f"\n❌ Invalid JSON format in '{file_path}': {e}")
    except ValidationError as e:
        # Catches Pydantic validation errors
        print("\n❌ Validation Failed!")
        print("Pydantic Validation Errors:")
        print(e)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    file_path = "agent_card.json"
    validate_agent_card(file_path)

```

discussion_title: Discussion: Should Message events terminate the event stream? #248
author: questflow-kyle
raw_text:
# Message Events Should Not Terminate Event Streams

## Current Behavior
The event stream terminates when receiving any Message event, effectively limiting agents to sending a single message per task execution.

```python
# Python: EventConsumer.consume_all()
is_final_event = (... or isinstance(event, Message) ...)

# TypeScript: ExecutionEventQueue.events()
if (event.kind === 'message' || ...) { break; }
```

## Use Cases We Need

Many real-world scenarios require streaming multiple messages and artifacts:

```
message: <Chunk1>
message: <Chunk2>
message: <Chunk3>
artifact: <Chunk1>
artifact: <Chunk2>
artifact: <Chunk3>
```

Examples:
- **Streaming LLM responses**: Long-form content generation with real-time updates
- **Multi-step workflows**: Progress updates during complex operations
- **Tool-calling patterns**: Back-and-forth communication for clarifications
- **Mixed content streams**: Alternating between messages and artifacts

## Why This Design is Problematic

1. **Inconsistent with Artifact behavior**: Artifacts support streaming with `firstChunk`/`lastChunk`, but Messages don't
2. **Forces workarounds**: Developers must use artifacts for simple text streaming, which is semantically incorrect
3. **Limits agent capabilities**: Cannot implement common patterns like progress reporting or interactive workflows
4. **Poor developer experience**: Counter-intuitive that sending a message terminates the entire stream

## Proposal

### 1. Add Streaming Support to Messages
Similar to Artifacts, add `firstChunk` and `lastChunk` fields to Message:

```typescript
interface Message {
  text: string;
  firstChunk?: boolean;
  lastChunk?: boolean;
}
```

### 2. Change Termination Logic
Only terminate the stream when:
- `TaskStatusUpdateEvent.final = true` is received
- Or when a Message/Artifact with `lastChunk = true` is received

### 3. Why Now is the Right Time
The A2A protocol currently has a very small audience and is still in early stages. Making this breaking change now will:
- Prevent future migration issues
- Establish better patterns from the start
- Avoid fragmenting the ecosystem with workarounds

## Questions for Discussion

1. Is there a specific reason why Messages were designed to be terminal events?
2. Would adding `firstChunk`/`lastChunk` to Messages align with the protocol's vision?

discussion_title: Implementing AWS SigV4 via AgentExtension?
author: LucaButBoring
raw_text:
I'm looking to host A2A servers on AWS and wanted to use IAM to scope caller permissions. Previously, this wasn't possible, but with `AgentExtension` it sounds like this might be doable – the docs on [Required Extensions](https://a2aproject.github.io/A2A/0.2.3/topics/extensions/#required-extensions) even call out message signing as an explicit use case.

Unfortunately, there doesn't yet appear to be any clear language describing what extensions are allowed to do at a low level, so it's hard to see if I would be able to implement [SigV4](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_sigv-create-signed-request.html) on them right now.

I'd like to know three things about extensions in particular:
1. Where in the request lifecycle are extensions allowed to be invoked? I'd need to be able to sign an entire request and add four HTTP headers to it ([docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_sigv-authentication-methods.html)) just prior to the request being sent.
2. What modifications can extensions make to an HTTP request? As noted in (1), I'd need to add multiple arbitrary headers to a request prior to sending it.
3. Do extensions need to be implemented in multiple languages? While I assume this is the case, there's nothing suggesting how language-specific extensions might be discovered from a URI and loaded, so it's unclear how extensions will have a way of pointing to specific sources on e.g. NPM/PyPI that should be used, or if there's going to be some language-independent way of loading extensions instead.

discussion_title: Implementing AWS SigV4 via AgentExtension?
author: mikeas1
raw_text:
Hey, sorry for missing this question. I think this is a great use case for extensions.

Let me try to answer your questions:

> 1. Where in the request lifecycle are extensions allowed to be invoked? I'd need to be able to sign an entire request and add four HTTP headers to it ([docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_sigv-authentication-methods.html)) just prior to the request being sent.

Technically, anywhere. The Python SDK support I plan to add will be based on lifecycle hooks, and adding a hook that alters the HTTP content of an outgoing request is a good fit.  This even seems like a reasonable fit for a standard `Auth` handler for an httpx client, if you're using Python. Looks like there are even existing libraries for it: https://github.com/Colin-b/httpx_auth#aws-signature-v4

> 2. What modifications can extensions make to an HTTP request? As noted in (1), I'd need to add multiple arbitrary headers to a request prior to sending it.

Since A2A doesn't strictly dictate HTTP headers, this is allowed. The primary restriction for extensions is that they cannot modify the core types in the specification (by adding new fields or removing required fields).

> 3. Do extensions need to be implemented in multiple languages? While I assume this is the case, there's nothing suggesting how language-specific extensions might be discovered from a URI and loaded, so it's unclear how extensions will have a way of pointing to specific sources on e.g. NPM/PyPI that should be used, or if there's going to be some language-independent way of loading extensions instead.

At the moment, yes. Extensions are not currently setup to be loaded on-demand -- you must directly configure the set of extensions your agent or client supports. This means it's the responsibility of the developer to identify the language-specific packages that implement the extension, then configure those in whatever way the package defines.

Extensions are still very new, so I expect some of this to change, and hopefully get easier. My goal is that the SDK-level support for extensions becomes as straightforward as:
```python
handler = DefaultRequestHandler(
  # ...
  extensions=[AWSSigV4Extension(aws_credential)],
)
```

discussion_title: Implementing AWS SigV4 via AgentExtension?
author: LucaButBoring
raw_text: Got it, this answers most of my questions, thanks! Regarding extension loading then (since it's lang-specific) - are there any planned ways for server agents to point clients to appropriate extension packages that client agents should be using, either through standardized error messages etc.? Requiring the client agent developer to explicitly install a client-side extension in their agent is reasonable, I'm just interested in what discovery might look like in that circumstance.

discussion_title: Implementing AWS SigV4 via AgentExtension?
author: mikeas1
raw_text: Nothing directly machine readable at this point. Since extensions are identified by a URI, my expectation is that extension developers will have that be a URL that points to documentation. They can then include links to implementation packages in their docs. Otherwise, you'd use normal search means for finding implementations of published protocols (i.e. google or npm/pypi/whatever search).

discussion_title: Typing of DataPart
author: sfitts
raw_text:
If I'm reading the definition of the *DataPart* schema correctly, then the `data` property is typed as a JSON object.  This leads to a couple of questions:

* The comment in the schema says, "The structured JSON data payload (an object or an array)."  Given the declared type this does not appear to be true.  Is this simply an error in the comment or was the intent that `data` should be allowed to be either an object or an array.
* Why isn't `data` typed as to allow any JSON root element?  Once upon a time JSON restricted the root to object and array, but that hasn't been true since RFC 7159.

Assuming we are restricted to just producing JSON objects, what is the intended way to transmit other outputs?  Should an array be broken down into one *Part* per item?  What if the items are not themselves JSON object values?  Sorry if this is all obvious, but I couldn't find any clear explanation of the intent here.

discussion_title: Typing of DataPart
author: DracoBlue
raw_text:
One advantage of using the data as dictionary - with a `string` key and value as `any` typed - is if you think about having multiple `DataPart`s appended for the same artefact.

Think of streaming the flight number and airline as:

```
{
  "no": "AIR123",
  "operator": "Air Line"
}
```

and giving the price a little bit later like this:

```
{
  "price": "120",
}
```

... this makes it possible to merge the resulting data. If it wouldn't be a dictionary - you would have to define how appending (merging?!) them should look like.

IMHO having it as dictionary is fine. If you want to put anything else into it give it a good key :). What do you think?

discussion_title: Typing of DataPart
author: sfitts
raw_text: I tend to feel that it is a bit too restrictive/arbitrary.  We want to use the agent artifacts to represent elements from the underlying application domain (which for us might be anything) and it is not uncommon to have artifacts which are not naturally represented as dictionaries.  Forcing the agent to always wrap these, while possible, ends up being extra work for both the agent and the client (since the artifact is neither produced nor consumed as a dictionary).  Hope that makes some sense.

discussion_title: Authenticated Knowledge & Trust Architecture for A2A Agents , AKTA.
author: lukehinds
raw_text:
Sharing a prototype project I built called "Akta"

https://github.com/RedDotRocket/akta

It's an attempt to enable secure and verifiable auth and delegation between AI agents. It establishes a framework for time-bound capability-based access control, allowing agents to delegate tasks and share resources with fine-grained control. The system leverages concepts from Decentralised Identifiers (DIDs) and Verifiable Credentials (VCs) to create a cryptographically and auditable chain of trust for autonomous agent operations. 

The skills and capabilities used to generate the cryptographic verifiable credential are gathered from an Agents A2A card.
In essence, Akta tries to answer what does a "fully autonomous Agent to Agent authorisation grant look like with no humans in the loop"? a.k.a an Agent delegating tasks to another Agent of their own accord. The human presence is derived from their position higher up the chain to their Agents (and the agents they delegate to). There is also a CLI and library for creating keys, vc's, based on A2A AgentCards and their nominated capabilities and skillz!

If you are interested in this idea and want to hack on it with me, let me know. Typical me style, I have way too many uncompleted projects and I am focusing on getting out my main one over the next few weeks. But I do love all this DID stuff and my heart is in this tech, so hopefully this is valuable to someone one out there

FYI, this is prototype, it has no biz being near production and is not audited yet, its a proof of concept that I might develop futher.

discussion_title: A Network-Layer Pointer for AgentCards (via DNS)
author: nembal
raw_text:
Hey everyone,

I've been following the excellent discussion on signing `AgentCard`s in thread #199. The focus on using Sigstore, DSSE, etc., to verify the integrity and authenticity of a card's *content* is absolutely critical.

This got me thinking about the step just before that: how do we trust the *location* of the card itself?

Right now, the convention is to find the card at a `/.well-known/agent.json` URI. But as pointed out in the thread, what if that location is compromised?

This suggests a layered approach to trust. We need to trust the **path** to the card, and then we need to trust the **content** of the card. The signing discussion is solving the second part. I think DNS is the right tool for the first.

I've formalized a simple spec called **Agent Interface Discovery (AID)** that uses a DNS `TXT` record to act as a secure, network-layer pointer to an agent's interface.

For A2A, it would look like this:
```dns
_agent.example.com. IN TXT "v=aid1;p=a2a;uri=https://example.com/agent.json"
```

Here’s how this complements the work on signing:

1.  **Trusting the Path:** By signing this DNS record with **DNSSEC**, a domain owner can create a strong, cryptographic guarantee that they have designated `https://example.com/agent.json` as their official `AgentCard` location. A client can verify this *before* making a single HTTP request.

2.  **Trusting the Content:** The client then fetches the `AgentCard` from that verified URI. It can *then* use the mechanisms being discussed in #199 (Sigstore, DSSE) to verify the integrity of the JSON content itself.

Together, this creates a powerful chain of trust, from the network layer all the way to the application layer.

AID's philosophy is to be the "stupidly simple" signpost, not the blueprint. The `AgentCard` is the blueprint, and it does that job perfectly. AID just provides the verified street address.

This makes A2A agents first-class citizens of the internet, discoverable with the same robustness as email servers. It feels like the right primitive to build a truly decentralized and autonomous agent ecosystem upon.

The spec is finalized and ready to use. Would love to hear what the A2A community thinks about this layered approach to discovery and trust.

**Learn more and see the workbench here: [aid.agentcommunity.org](https://aid.agentcommunity.org)**

discussion_title: A2A + MQTT?
author: architectureofthings
raw_text: Thoughts on integrating A2A and MQTT in a coherent architecture?

discussion_title: A2A + MQTT?
author: mikeas1
raw_text:
MQTT -> Agent (push based): an MQTT-A2A bridge could make sense, since A2A is a push-based protocol (i.e. you send agents messages directly). The bridge subscribes to MQTT topics you're interested in and is configured with an AgentCard to send events to. A minimal bridge could just package the message payload as a [FilePart](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/#66-filebase-object) (which allows specifying a content type) containing the base64-encoded bytes of the payload. This is the "Hey agent, here's some data, go do your thing" option.

Agent -> MQTT (pull based): An MCP server for subscribing and publishing. The agent could directly pull and inspect events, then push new events via tools. You invoke the agent somehow to get it to wake up and use its tools: "It's been 10 seconds, check if there's anything else interesting to do".

Hybrid: Equip an agent with tools for inspecting and publishing events, then invoke the agent when a new event arrives. This just acts as a signal to the agent, it doesn't pass the data directly: "new events are available, go do your thing".

Just my 2 cents on it. It would be neat to feed all your local events into an agent and have it do something useful.

discussion_title: A2A + MQTT?
author: andreibesleaga
raw_text:
Could be used same as Agentic AI with other streaming/pubsub systems (Kafka), but with the limitations/specifics of MQTT.


There might be a few ways this could be supported in practice, integrated in different architectures types:

1. MQTT as a Transport Layer: A2A messages (e.g., tasks/send, tasks/status) are wrapped and sent via topics instead of HTTP. This requires minimal changes to the message format but shifts the infrastructure underneath, as described above.
2. MQTT for Task Routing and Fan-Out: Keep direct A2A communication for core task execution (as is HTTPS), but publish all task submissions, updates, and artifacts to MQTT in parallel. Other systems can then react to these events in real time. (commands only).
3. Hybrid Orchestration Pattern: Use MQTT to drive workflows involving multiple agents. An orchestrator listens for events and sends A2A tasks to downstream agents. This combines A2A’s interactions with MQTT.
4. Adapted specifically to MQTT architecture and usage: both as a transport layer and for real-time embedded connected (IoT) systems which might implement the agents themselves.

In the diagram on the A2A main page (https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/#a2a-and-mcp-complementary-protocols) it shows a section called "local agents", which I take to mean a series of agents all deployed on the same system. discussion_title: Communication between two local agents - how?
author: darrelmiller
raw_text: One of the advantages of using HTTPS as the underlying protocol for all the A2A transports and a URL for the Agent identifier is that it provides location independence.  Two agents can communicate if they are on the same machine or if they are continents away.  The one exception here is the use of .well-known as the agent card discovery mechanism.  It is currently not possible to have two agents on the same logical host name.  However, use of .well-known is optional.

discussion_title: Communication between two local agents - how?
author: lrosenthol
raw_text:
@darrelmiller All of that is true in the larger server-side context of agents....But thinking about how local device agents will operate, I think it introduces more problems than it solves.

- Each agent will need to expose an https server on some arbitrary port number, which is now open on that device for ANYONE (e.g., bad actor) to go after.  Additionally, those agents will have to deal with port conflicts, which leads us to...
- How do you register an agent for discovery on a device, since as you note, `.well-known` won't work?  And how can you do it, if you don't know the available port until you start it up?  Looks like this one already has an open issue tracking it - https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/issues/378
-  HTTPS for localhost doesn't work well in many cases, since the common solution is to use a self-signed cert...but what you really want is a cert specific to that agent's "owner" (just as you have for the web-server case).

discussion_title: Agent, Skill, and (Intent) Routing
author: ognis1205
raw_text: ## Challenges in Scaling Natural Language Interfaces in A2A When building A2A-compatible agents that accept natural language input and route it to the appropriate AgentSkill, we inevitably face challenges as the number of skills grows. While A2A encourages flexible interaction via prompts, scaling the number of skills brings forth several concrete issues around routing accuracy, ambiguity, and performance: ### Specific Challenges Emerging with a Growing Number of Skills 1. Increased Ambiguity - For example, when a user says "Make a reservation," is it for a room, a restaurant, or a flight? - Even the same phrase may have different meanings across skills. 2. Overlapping Skills - Skills like "Summarize document" and "Analyze document" may both seem plausible given the same user intent, making disambiguation difficult. 3. Routing Accuracy Degradation - If skill definitions or expectations are vague, the agent might select the wrong one. - Longer or more context-dependent user inputs exacerbate this issue. --- ## Key Discussion Topics 1. Scoping intent recognition and skill selection to a specific Agent—rather than evaluating all skills globally—not only significantly reduces ambiguity and improves precision. This approach also suggests a broader architectural shift: while the A2A model currently (implicitly) assumes "one host, one agent," many real-world scenarios could benefit from supporting multiple agents under a single host. Each agent could expose its own scoped skill sets and interfaces. This modular partitioning helps manage large capabilities and mitigates scaling issues—especially when combined with catalog-based discovery mechanisms. 2. Another important topic is how to handle cases when no matching skill can be found. This raises the question of whether the A2A specification itself should define standardized error codes or response formats for such fallback scenarios. --- ## Extending Routing with Programmable Logic Some frameworks, such as LangChain Routing, support hybrid routing approaches that combine natural language intent recognition with programmable, declarative routing logic. This allows more structured and reliable delegation across tools or agents beyond purely free-form input interpretation. A key question for the A2A ecosystem is whether similar programmable routing capabilities can and should be defined at the protocol or usage level. Could the A2A specification be extended to support declarative routing policies, modular routers, or programmable dispatch? What benefits and challenges would such extensions entail for interoperability and flexibility? Additionally, since routing between Agents and AgentSkills may involve guardrails or constraints to ensure correct dispatch, should the protocol also define corresponding error types or codes to handle routing failures explicitly? Defining such error semantics could improve robustness and clarity in routing behaviors within the A2A ecosystem.

discussion_title: Agent, Skill, and (Intent) Routing
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
I received some very insightful feedback from a core community member, which clarified the nuanced nature of agent skill invocation and discovery.

## Skills Are Not APIs

They pointed out that agent delegation based on intent depends not only on the agent’s own prompting/model but also on how clearly the remote agent describes its behavior. Importantly, **skills are not APIs** — they should not be treated as contract-based endpoints. Instead, they are descriptions of what the agent can do, often loosely defined. This is why skills shouldn’t be directly requested or interpreted like API errors.

## Clarification via TaskState

Interestingly, the protocol already includes a mechanism enabling them to ask for clarification instead of proceeding with uncertain execution. They noted that this feature is underutilized but potentially powerful for enabling more dynamic and exploratory interactions:

 - [Life of a Task](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/topics/life-of-a-task)
 - [TaskState](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/#63-taskstate-enum)

## Scalability and Trust

They also acknowledged the challenges of scalability and quality in agent selection, noting that these will likely be addressed through context management strategies on the client side.

discussion_title: Agent, Skill, and (Intent) Routing
author: lukehinds
raw_text:
Unsure if its spec aligned but in [AgentUp](https://github.com/RedDotRocket/AgentUp) I map skills to Tools. Tools are registered as available for function calling by the model and in turn advertised as available within the Agent Card. 

So an agent might have a tool `get_location` which is part of an API that calls Google maps with long/lat and replies with correct up to date map data. That is a skill that the particular agent has. It could also in a way be an inherent ability of most models, but without the tool it could be stale or hallucinated, so its a differentiating skill. 

I can't really think of any skills an agent might have beyond Tools / MCP etc (or just plain ole APIs), perhaps the model itself? An agent might be multi-modal as it has a model capable of both text and image processing?

discussion_title: Agent, Skill, and (Intent) Routing
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Hi @lukehinds ,

Thanks for sharing your approach — I actually agree with much of your understanding and design philosophy when thinking about what an `AgentSkill` represents at the implementation level. In fact, this whole thread started from a very similar modeling approach on my end.

That said, I do think there are cases where modeling `AgentSkill`s strictly as Function Calling endpoints or MCP Tools may fall short. For example, in RAG-based Q&A agents, the interaction style is often exploratory and incomplete — more about trying to answer than precisely calling a tool. Restricting skills to pre-defined, tool-like constructs could make it harder to represent flexible, clarification-driven exchanges. (Which, interestingly, seems aligned with your point about multimodal agents — so I think we do share the same underlying concerns.)

In any case, from implementation perspective, I believe the current A2A `AgentSkill` abstraction will likely need to accommodate a broad spectrum — from guard-rails to Function Calling and/or MCP Tools to open-ended natural language handlers. It’s a deep and wide design space, and one we’ll probably have to refine collectively through usage and iteration across projects.

Also, perhaps it could be useful to eventually introduce a `type` field in the `AgentSkill` definition — something that indicates whether the skill is a `tool`, `interactive`, or some other category. That might help capture the range of interaction styles.

For what it’s worth, I’m also considering submitting a PR to the samples repo showing a case that leverages clarification, as I shared before in this thread, more explicitly — not sure if it’ll be accepted, but I think it could help surface some of these ideas.

P.S. I also had a look at the AgentUp project — haven’t explored everything yet, but it looks like a really useful framework. I’d love to learn more about it!

discussion_title: Agent, Skill, and (Intent) Routing
author: ognis1205
raw_text:
@lukehinds 

Just to clarify my previous comment:

I believe your model of mapping skills to tools in AgentUp project makes a lot of sense when the agent’s capabilities are well-defined by APIs or function calls (e.g., via MCP or similar). However, in use cases like RAG-based Q&A bots, the notion of a "skill" may not align 1:1 with tools — the skill might be emergent from the model’s retrieval or reasoning ability, without a discrete tool boundary. (Even in such cases, it might be possible to map them 1:1 to functions or tools, depending on how they are implemented or operationalized.)

That said, the actual implementation can flexibly determine how skills are handled, and I believe that’s why the specification intentionally leaves the definition of `AgentSkill` somewhat vague. Because of this, there may not be a single "correct" interpretation at the implementation or operational level; rather, what matters more is how the concept of a skill is understood in the context of agent-to-agent interactions and at the prompt or reasoning level.

discussion_title: A2A Agents Registry Platform: Comprehensive Proposal
author: ajaynagotha
raw_text:
My proposal is inspired by official docs: https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/topics/agent-discovery/

# A2A Agents Registry Platform Proposal

## Executive Summary

The **A2A Agents Registry Platform** aims to be a comprehensive, trusted, scalable, and standards-compliant directory and marketplace for Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol-compliant AI agents. This platform empowers agent providers to publish detailed agent profiles and metadata while enabling developers, enterprises, and integrators to effortlessly discover, evaluate, and integrate AI agents into their ecosystems.

By establishing rigorous registration and validation processes, advanced discovery features, transparent governance, and federation support, the platform accelerates collaborative AI adoption and helps build a vibrant multi-agent ecosystem.

## Core Requirements and Principles

### 1. Agent Registration Requirements

Providers **must** submit complete and accurate information including:

- **Agent Title**  
  Concise, clear name for easy identification.

- **Agent Description**  
  Detailed explanation of the agent’s purpose, capabilities, supported functionalities, and use cases.

- **Agent Card URL (Machine-readable Metadata)**  
  Publicly accessible JSON, JSON-LD, or DCAT document containing:
  - Supported capabilities and skills
  - Compatible A2A protocol versions
  - Endpoint URLs and operation interfaces
  - Authentication methods (OAuth, API keys, mTLS, etc.)
  - Pricing models or usage limits
  - Performance metrics (uptime, latency)
  - Versioning and compliance details

- **Comprehensive Documentation URL or Upload**  
  Human-readable guides covering:
  - Use cases and functions
  - Input/output data formats and schemas
  - Integration instructions including authentication setup
  - Version history and changelogs
  - Sample requests/responses
  - Known limitations and best practices

- **Usage Terms and Pricing Information**  
  Clear statement of whether the agent service is free or priced. Pricing details must include:
  - Pricing tiers
  - Pay-per-use or subscription models
  - Usage caps or limits

- **Contact Information or Access Portal Details**  
  Contacts such as email addresses, portal URLs for:
  - Access requests (API keys, onboarding)
  - Technical support and commercial negotiations

### 2. Interoperability

- Agents must strictly comply with the most current A2A protocol specifications.
- Agent Card schemas must follow evolving standards to facilitate automated discovery, trust verification, integration tooling, and federation support.
- Support JSON-RPC 2.0 over HTTP(S) with industry-standard authentication.

### 3. Discovery and Search Capabilities

- Advanced search filters by:
  - Agent capabilities and domains (e.g., finance, healthcare)
  - Supported protocol versions
  - Pricing and usage terms
  - Performance and availability
  - Provider reputation and user ratings

- Personalized recommendations based on user preferences and behavior.

### 4. Transparency and Trust

- Agents listed only after passing automated metadata validation and documentation quality checks.
- Display of real-time operational statuses.
- Verification of provider identities.
- Community-driven reviews, ratings, feedback, and moderation mechanisms.

### 5. Security and Access Control

- Support OAuth 2.0, API keys, mutual TLS, and other standard authentication methods.
- Fine-grained entitlement and access management to regulate visibility and capabilities per user or organization.
- Enforce secure communication channels.

### 6. Compliance and Quality Assurance

- Automated and manual validation pipelines to verify:
  - Metadata schema compliance
  - Documentation quality and completeness
  - Uptime, performance, and security standards

- Ongoing monitoring through user feedback, audits, and moderation.

- Compliance with data privacy regulations including GDPR, CCPA, etc.

### 7. Scalability and Federation

- Designed to operate as both a centralized registry and part of a federated ecosystem.
- Support interoperability and data exchange between trusted registries for broad agent discovery.

## Core Functionalities

### 1. Agent Discovery

- User-friendly interfaces with keyword and faceted search.
- Filtering by agent capabilities, pricing, availability, and reputation.
- Personalized recommendations.

### 2. Agent Profiles

- Rich pages displaying:
  - Agent Card metadata and documentation links
  - Usage terms and pricing
  - Contact/access info
  - Authentication requirements
  - User reviews and live agent status

### 3. Provider Dashboard

- Secure self-service portal for providers to:
  - Register and update agent profiles and metadata
  - Upload/revise documentation and pricing
  - Monitor usage analytics and compliance
  - Manage support and commercial inquiries

### 4. Developer Integration Suite

- Interactive API explorers for live testing.
- Auto-generated code samples and SDK snippets.
- Sandboxed environments for integration testing.
- Centralized developer documentation portals.

### 5. Community & Quality Assurance

- Verified rating and review systems.
- Discussion forums and Q&A.
- Moderation teams and automated flags to ensure quality and trust.

### 6. Enterprise Features

- Tools for vendor assessments, compliance checks, contract management.
- SLA tracking and enforcement.
- Advanced access controls for organizational governance.

## API Reference (Minimal Consensus-aligned)

- **`GET /agents`**  
  Returns a list of registered agents filtered by capabilities, pricing, availability, and reputation. Supports granular query parameters for filtering.

- **`GET /agents/{agent_id}`**  
  Retrieves full Agent Card metadata, human-readable documentation links, pricing and usage details, contact information, and live status for a specific agent.

- **`POST /agents/register`**  
  Authenticated endpoint for providers to register new agents or update existing ones. Payload must include all mandatory registration fields such as Agent Title, Description, Agent Card URL, Documentation URL, pricing terms, contact info, and supported authentication methods.

- **`GET /health`**  
  Returns the status and liveness information of the registry platform, useful for system monitoring.

## Submission and Listing Process

1. **Provider Verification**  
   Secure account creation with identity validation.

2. **Agent Registration**  
   Submission of comprehensive metadata, documentation, pricing, and contact details.

3. **Validation**  
   Automated schema and metadata checks, plus manual quality and security reviews as needed.

4. **Approval & Listing**  
   Approved agents become publicly discoverable with transparent usage and access terms.

5. **Ongoing Updates**  
   Providers update agent information as needed to maintain accuracy via the dashboard.

## Governance and Compliance

- Enforce content quality, security standards, and operational reliability.
- Data privacy compliance (GDPR, CCPA, others).
- Transparent moderation with dispute resolution.
- Regular audits and community feedback integration.

## Success Metrics

- Number and quality of fully registered agents.
- Growth in developer, enterprise, and integrator engagement.
- Uptake and operational uptime of integrated agents.
- Positive audit results for quality and compliance.
- Provider and user satisfaction and trust indicators.

## Community Collaboration and Contribution Invitation

To build this platform successfully, broad community involvement is essential. Collaboration opportunities include:

- Refining Agent Card schema standards.
- Developing validation, security, and federation tools.
- Designing intuitive UIs for discovery and provider dashboards.
- Building community moderation, rating, and reputation systems.
- Exploring cross-registry federation protocols.

Your feedback and contributions are welcome to help bring this vision to life.

## Conclusion

The **A2A Agents Registry Platform** provides the vital infrastructure for transparent, secure, and efficient discovery, evaluation, and integration of A2A protocol-compliant AI agents. By requiring comprehensive machine-readable and human-readable information, transparent pricing and usage terms, along with strong validation and governance, it encourages trust and collaboration in a rapidly evolving multi-agent AI ecosystem.

Will share initial working prototype once it is ready from my side.
